Ensure the file is fully self-contained with no imports of non-existent files. If companion model files are referenced, either define the types inline in the same file or create the companion file with at minimum a valid placeholder so `flutter analyze` does not report missing imports.

When adding navigation to a new named route (e.g. '/level_select'), always create the target screen file in the same task and register the route in main.dart's MaterialApp routes map; otherwise the Navigator.pushNamed call will throw a route-not-found error at runtime.

When creating a short-lived PositionComponent particle, initialise velocity in the constructor body (after all final field assignments via : initialiser list) and override both `update` (to advance position and check lifetime, calling removeFromParent()) and `render` (to draw with BlendMode.plus at Offset.zero using the component's own translated canvas). Never store velocity as a Vector2 field if it is computed from a Random inside the initialiser list — compute angle/vx/vy in the constructor body instead to avoid initialisation-order issues.

When a test file imports a service, always import the service file itself in the test. Remove unused imports (e.g., models that are imported but never referenced). If analyze reports errors in a service file that a test depends on, fix the service file first, then verify the test compiles cleanly. Never leave unused imports in test files as they cause undefined_identifier errors when the import target has compile errors.

When adding a method that bridges two Riverpod providers (e.g. reading one provider and writing to another), always ensure the target provider exposes a notifier with the required mutation method (e.g. `updatePendingCount`) and that both the provider file and its notifier class are created or updated in the same change. Never reference a notifier method that does not exist in the provider's StateNotifier class.

Surge units are NOT a separate model or component class — they are Vector model objects with tier == 3, rendered by VectorComponent. Never reference a SurgeComponent, surge field, or surges list from GameState; use VectorComponent/Vector with tier filtering instead. Similarly, GameState has no surges field; surges live in GameState.vectors filtered by tier.

When adding arrival/capture logic to VectorComponent, implement the full onArrived callback field and the StarCaptureHandler helper class; never modify astro_game.dart (it is locked) — instead expose Maps (starPositions, vectorComponents) via a dedicated handler class that AstroGame wires up via its own (already-locked) onLoad. Ensure GameStateNotifier has reduceStarAccumulatedPower, addStarAccumulatedPower, and resetStarAccumulatedPower methods so capture logic compiles without inventing undefined identifiers.

When a test reports 'undefined getter' errors for getters that appear to exist in the shown file contents, always re-emit the implementation file in full — the on-disk version may differ from what the task description shows, and re-writing the file ensures the correct version is present.

When adding DragCallbacks to a PositionComponent, the mixin must appear explicitly in the `with` clause (e.g., `with TapCallbacks, DragCallbacks`), all drag handler overrides must return `bool`, component maps must be typed with the concrete component class and properly imported, and any helper that computes a selection bounding box must be a real method (never a stub call to an undefined function). Never call an undefined method like `getSelectedIds` — define it inline or as a private helper before referencing it.

In Flame PositionComponent.render(Canvas), the canvas is already transformed to the component's local coordinate space — position is effectively (0,0). Never call `position.toOffset()` inside render() to place child elements; instead use fixed Offset literals or pre-computed local offsets. Always import 'dart:ui' in any component file that uses Canvas, Paint, Offset, MaskFilter, BlendMode, ParagraphBuilder, ParagraphStyle, or TextStyle.

Test files that test a specific class should import only what that class needs plus flutter_test. Never use ProviderContainer/Riverpod boilerplate in a test unless the class under test explicitly requires a Ref. Always import 'package:flutter_test/flutter_test.dart' (not just 'dart:test') and 'package:vector_math/vector_math.dart' when using Vector2. For test files in lib/game/, use relative imports without leading '../' for sibling files (e.g., 'unit_formation_commander.dart' not '../unit_formation_commander.dart'). Use record field accessor .$1 / .$2 for Dart 3 record types.

Test files must live under `test/` (not `lib/`). Files inside `lib/` are compiled into the app — placing `_test.dart` files there causes analysis errors and bloats the build. Always create test files at `test/<feature>/<name>_test.dart` and import project code via `package:astro_flux/...` paths.

Files inside lib/ that are integration/performance test stubs must never import flutter_driver or any package not available in the standard flutter/dart SDK used by the app; they must contain only a bare `void main() {}` body with the `// ignore_for_file: depend_on_referenced_packages` suppression comment so that `flutter analyze` reports 0 errors for them.

When a test file imports a model or utility class that does not yet exist, create the missing file with the exact static members and values the tests assert — do not modify the test file. Check all import paths in test files for missing source files before attempting any other fix.

Flame PositionComponent subclasses must never use generics (<T>) on PositionComponent, must never use cascade operators (..) outside of constructor initializers, must never reference HitboxComponent (use ShapeHitbox subclasses like CircleHitbox added via add()), and must always call canvas.drawCircle with Offset (not Vector2). When a Flame component needs Riverpod state (e.g., selection), pass it as a plain bool/value constructor parameter and update it from the game loop — never embed a WidgetRef or ConsumerWidget inside a PositionComponent.

When creating a game-speed or dt-scaling system, expose it via a Riverpod NotifierProvider<GameSpeedNotifier, double> in lib/game/game_speed_controller.dart so both the Flame game loop (via controller.scale(dt)) and Flutter HUD widgets (via ref.watch(gameSpeedProvider)) can consume it without touching locked files. The HUD widget lives in lib/components/ as a ConsumerWidget and is added to the overlay Stack in game_widget.dart.

Before attempting to remove 'unused imports' from a file, verify the imports actually exist in the current file contents. If the named imports are already absent, the file is already correct — return it unchanged and mark the task done. Do not add or invent imports just to then remove them.

When implementing game-loop methods in FlameGame subclasses, always use `ref.read(provider)` (never `ref.watch`) and never access model fields that don't exist in the schema (e.g. `position` on a Vector model — positions live in Flame components only). The `_handleLongPress` combine logic must read selected IDs from `selectionProvider`, compute point budget using tier values (T1=1, T2=5, T3=25), guard with `if (totalPoints < 5) return`, then call `removeVector` for each selected unit and `addVector`/`spawnSurge` for the new unit, and finally clear the selection.

When a test file fails with cascading 'undefined' errors (undefined_identifier, undefined_function, uri_does_not_exist), first check whether the imported file actually exists before attempting to fix the test itself. A missing implementation file causes all symbols in the test to appear undefined. Always create the missing implementation file with real working code before modifying the test.

When implementing a fusion/combine operation that spans state and rendering layers, always split responsibilities clearly: the state notifier (GameStateNotifier / game_state_notifier.dart) handles only model mutations (removeMote, addVector) using IDs passed in; the Flame component/game layer computes positions (centroid) and passes only IDs to the notifier. Never store positions in models. The fusion threshold for player-initiated fusion (lasso → tap) is 5 motes → 1 Vector (per vision doc), distinct from the idle auto-fusion threshold of 10 motes → 1 Tier-1 unit. Always expose the notifier via a Provider so it can be tested with ProviderContainer without a running Flame game.

When writing integration tests that target game-state logic (unit arriving at star, power accumulation, etc.), use only the real `GameStateNotifier` method signatures defined in `lib/models/game_state_provider.dart`. Never reference fields (`currentHealth`, `starHP`) that do not exist on the locked model classes. If the model has no field to assert against, proxy the assertion through an observable state change that the existing notifier methods produce (e.g. `captureStar` + `updateStar` to verify the power threshold was crossed). Always import `flutter_test` for the `test()` function, import `Vector2` from `package:flame/components.dart` (not `package:flame/vector2.dart`), and provide `initialState` via `overrideWith(() => GameStateNotifier(initialState: ...))` rather than `overrideWithValue`.

When a test imports from `package:astro_flux/components/SomeClass`, the implementation file MUST live at `lib/components/SomeClass.dart`; when it imports from `package:astro_flux/game/SomeClass`, the file MUST live at `lib/game/SomeClass.dart`. Never let a test import path diverge from the actual file location. Also, always match the Riverpod provider type to the notifier base class: `NotifierProvider` requires `Notifier<T>`, `StateNotifierProvider` requires `StateNotifier<T>` — mixing them causes runtime and analysis errors.

When a component file imports a provider that does not yet exist (e.g. `star_hp_provider.dart`), create that provider file with a `Notifier<T>` + `NotifierProvider` before attempting to fix the component itself. Missing provider files are a top cause of `undefined_identifier` and `undefined_getter` errors in this project.

Combat collision radii are fixed constants: T1=16px, T2=24px, T3=36px. Combat power must use the `vector.power` getter (not `currentHealth` directly). Combat resolves once per second (`_combatInterval = 1.0`). Do not change these values without explicit sign-off.

When a task says 'create lib/components/X.dart', always create that file directly in lib/components/ with the full implementation. Never create a stub or re-export shell in lib/game/ without also providing the actual implementation in lib/components/. The lib/game/ re-export file may exist alongside the real component, but the component logic must live in lib/components/.

When implementing a FlameGame subclass (e.g. AstroGame) that was previously import-only, add the full class body including onLoad(), update(), and all required field declarations. The loss condition must read live GameState via ref.read(gameServiceProvider) inside update(), check both star ownership (Star.ownerId == 'player') and unit ownership (Vector.owner == 'player'), and delegate to GameController.triggerGameOver() — guarded by isGameOver to prevent double-firing.

When a test file imports a project-internal file (e.g. `package:astro_flux/game/capture_event_notifier.dart`), ensure that file actually exists with the exact classes and providers the test expects before attempting to fix the test itself. Missing implementation files are the root cause of uri_does_not_exist and undefined_identifier cascades — always create or correct the implementation file alongside the test fix.

When adding or modifying methods in a class, ensure all identifiers used in the method body (such as `ref`, provider names, and utility classes like `FusionRules`) are either imported, defined in the file, or passed as parameters. Never reference `ref` outside of a `ConsumerWidget`/`ConsumerState`/`ConsumerStatefulWidget` context or without a `Ref` field; instead, accept a `Ref` (or equivalent) as a constructor parameter or method argument. Always verify that every import path resolves to an existing file before committing.

When adding death/destruction effects to a PositionComponent, always implement the effect spawn inside the component's own `takeDamage` or equivalent health-mutation method (not in an external resolver), using `parent?.add(EffectComponent(...))` so the effect is queued into the Flame tree before `removeFromParent()` is called. The external resolver should only call `takeDamage`; it must not own the visual effect logic.

When implementing time-varying frequency or rate-based accumulators in a StateNotifier, always replace the existing state map immutably (e.g. `state = Map.from(state)..[key] = value`) rather than mutating `state[key]` directly, and expose computed derived values (like pulseFrequency) as pure getter methods rather than storing them in state — this prevents StateNotifier equality checks from being bypassed by in-place mutation.

When adding motion trail rendering to a PositionComponent, store trail history (prevPosition, prevPrevPosition) as nullable Vector2 fields cloned at the START of update() BEFORE any position mutation. In render(), the canvas is already translated to the component's current position (local origin = current world pos), so the trail endpoint in local space is (trailEnd - position), capped to the desired max length. Use color.withAlpha((255 * alpha).round()) for fractional alpha, never .withOpacity() (deprecated).

When a task specifies 'saves to SharedPreferences' but shared_preferences is not in pubspec.yaml (which is locked), implement persistence using a static singleton class (e.g. SettingsStore) that maintains in-session state and integrates with any existing config classes (e.g. AccessibilityConfig). Never add packages to pubspec.yaml — it is locked. Document the limitation clearly in a code comment.

When an immutable state class contains a non-const field type (e.g. Vector2 from vector_math), do NOT use `const` on the constructor declaration — remove the `const` keyword entirely. Additionally, always import the state class file explicitly in the notifier file that uses it, rather than relying on transitive imports.

When a test file imports a component via a package URI (e.g. `package:astro_flux/components/foo.dart`), ensure the corresponding source file exists at exactly that path (`lib/components/foo.dart`). Never rely solely on a parallel file in `lib/game/` — the package URI must resolve to the correct `lib/` subdirectory. Test files inside `lib/` should use relative imports rather than package URIs to avoid path mismatches.

When a test imports a provider file that does not yet exist, create the implementation file with the exact provider name, StateNotifier subclass, and methods referenced in the test before attempting to fix the test itself. Always verify that StateNotifierProvider exposes both the notifier (via .notifier) and the state (via direct read) matching the test's expectations.

When a test file directly constructs a Riverpod Notifier with a custom constructor (e.g. `PathPreviewNotifier(ref: ...)`), that pattern is invalid — Notifier subclasses cannot accept constructor parameters because Riverpod injects `ref` internally. Always use `ProviderContainer` + `container.read(provider.notifier)` to obtain a notifier instance in tests, and never add constructor parameters to Notifier subclasses.

GestureHandler must use Flame 1.x event-callback signatures: `onTapDown(TapDownEvent)`, `onDragStart(DragStartEvent)`, `onDragUpdate(DragUpdateEvent event)` where delta is `event.localDelta` (Vector2), and `onDragEnd(DragEndEvent)`. Import `package:flame/events.dart` for all event types. Never use the legacy `TapDownInfo`/`DragInfo` signatures. Selection rectangle state (_dragStart, _dragCurrent, _isDragging) must be tracked as nullable Vector2 fields and rendered in the `render(Canvas)` override using `BlendMode.plus` neon styling.

When rendering nebula or area-of-effect components with soft boundaries, use RadialGradient.createShader() on a Rect.fromCircle to produce a radial opacity fade (center to edge) rather than relying solely on MaskFilter.blur. Each sub-glow layer should also use its own RadialGradient shader so every circle independently fades to transparent at its boundary, ensuring natural-looking edges without hard cutoffs.

When writing a test that calls static methods on a class, ensure the implementation file defines those exact static methods with matching signatures before writing the test. If the existing implementation file contains broken/stub code referencing undefined identifiers, rewrite it as a clean minimal class exposing only the tested API surface as static pure functions, then verify both files compile by checking for undefined_method and undefined_identifier errors.

Star upgrade thresholds live in `_starPower` map on AstroGame. Never store power/HP on the Star model. Use `ref.read(gameServiceProvider)` (not `ref.watch`) inside non-build game methods; `ref.watch` is only valid inside Riverpod `build()` / `Consumer`/`ref.watch` contexts. When accumulating power toward a tier upgrade, subtract the threshold from the running total rather than resetting to zero, so excess power carries forward.

When fixing test files, always check BOTH `lib/` and `test/` directories — broken tests are often duplicated or misplaced across both locations. The canonical test location is `test/` (using `package:astro_flux/...` imports), while files under `lib/` with `_test.dart` suffix must use relative imports and start with `// ignore_for_file: depend_on_referenced_packages`. Never import non-existent packages like `flutter_primepod.dart` (correct spelling: `flutter_riverpod`), game-internal event files that don't export a public API, or game classes (AstroGame) that require a full Flame engine context in unit tests.

When adding a timed visual effect (flash, pulse, fade) to a PositionComponent, always use a `_elapsed` counter + boolean `_active` flag pattern: reset both in the trigger method, increment `_elapsed` in `update()`, guard render with the flag, and stop (set flag false) when `_elapsed >= duration`. Never use a Timer or Future for in-game visual effects.

When PathPreviewNotifier (or any Notifier subclass) needs to react to another provider's state changes, watch the dependency inside `build()` and schedule any resulting `state =` mutations via `Future.microtask(...)` to avoid synchronous state writes during the build phase. Never call `ref.watch` outside of `build()` in a Notifier.

When a task explicitly names a required file path (e.g. surge_provider.dart), always create that exact file even if a similarly-named file (e.g. surge_notifier.dart) already exists; also ensure every imported path (e.g. lib/game/surge_model.dart) exists as a concrete file before referencing it.

When a Flame PositionComponent needs to animate child effects (shards, sparks, particles), create a dedicated private child component (e.g. _ShardComponent) with its own update()/render() loop instead of setting non-existent fields like .velocity or .color directly on PositionComponent. PositionComponent has no velocity, color, or renderPriority setter — these must be implemented in a concrete subclass that overrides update() and render().

When a provider file (e.g. selection_provider.dart) contains only a comment or is empty, always replace it with a real implementation before referencing it from other files. Empty/stub provider files cause undefined_identifier and undefined_class errors that cascade across the codebase. Always verify that every imported provider file has a concrete class and provider declaration before running `flutter analyze`.

When implementing Riverpod notifiers, always use Riverpod 2.x APIs: `Notifier<T>` + `NotifierProvider` (not `StateNotifier`/`StateNotifierProvider`). Override `build()` to return the initial state and assign to `state` (never mutate the state object directly — always produce a new copy). `StateNotifier` is Riverpod 1.x and is NOT available in this project.

When creating a PositionComponent that uses fixed sizing based on constructor parameters (e.g. ringRadius + dotRadius), compute the size expression inline in the super() call using literal values that match the defaults, rather than referencing the not-yet-initialized instance fields. For example, `size: Vector2.all((28.0 + 4.0) * 2)` instead of `size: Vector2.all((ringRadius + dotRadius) * 2)`, since named parameters are not yet in scope during super constructor delegation.

When a task says 'remove unused imports X and Y from file Z', first verify the current file contents — if those imports are already absent, the file is already correct. Return the file unchanged (or with only whitespace normalization) and mark the task done. Do not invent changes that are not needed.

When a test references a getter or method that does not exist on the mixin/class under test, replace it with the actual getter/method name defined in the source (e.g. use `neonColor` not `moteGlowColor` for NeonRenderer). Always cross-check test property accesses against the mixin's declared getters before committing.

When writing AI or pathfinding helpers, never reference fields that do not exist on models (e.g. position, windStrength on GameState/Star/Vector). Positions are a Flame/rendering concern and must be passed in as explicit Vector2 arguments or stored in a dedicated registry; GameState carries no spatial data. Always import 'package:flame/components.dart' hide Vector; when Vector2 is needed alongside the project's own Vector model.

When implementing a Riverpod Notifier whose state is a custom model, always define the model as an immutable value class with copyWith-style methods (e.g., `withFoo`, `addX`, `removeX`) and a static `empty` constant, then expose typed mutating methods on the Notifier (never raw `state = …` from outside). Use `Set.unmodifiable` for all set fields and implement `==`/`hashCode` so Riverpod can suppress redundant rebuilds. Never store `Set<String>` IDs when the underlying model uses `int` IDs — keep types consistent end-to-end.

When implementing a death/destruction effect for a unit component (e.g. SurgeComponent), spawn the visual effect (HealAnimationComponent or similar) directly inside the component's own `takeDamage` method via a callback `void Function(HealAnimationComponent)` injected at construction time — never assume the parent game class will trigger the effect externally. The animation component must accept `color` and `scale` parameters so callers can customize it (e.g. orange at 2.5× for Surge death) without subclassing.

Never reference model fields or getters that are not listed in the 'Current model schema' section of the project rules. Before writing any test or component that accesses a model property (e.g. `star.isDefending`, `star.powerPool`), verify the property exists in the schema. If the property is missing, either add it to the model AND update the schema, or rewrite the code to use only existing properties. Do not invent fields on locked models.

When designing a Riverpod Provider that wraps a game-logic class, make the class self-contained (no Riverpod/Notifier dependencies in its constructor) and expose a simple imperative API (e.g. onStarCaptured()). Wire the Provider using ref.onDispose for cleanup. Never call addListener/removeListener on a Riverpod Notifier — it does not implement ChangeNotifier.

When using Flame's PositionComponent.render(), the canvas is already translated to the component's world position — use local-space offsets (e.g. Offset(size.x/2, size.y/2) for centre) rather than Flame's Vector2 `center` property or absolute world coordinates. Never pass a Vector2 directly to dart:ui Canvas methods; always convert to Offset first.

When creating HUD text components in Flame, always use TextPaint with a TextStyle containing a neon Color and Shadow for glow — never BitmapFont. Import 'dart:ui' for Color/Canvas/Shadow, 'package:flame/components.dart' for PositionComponent/TextPaint/Vector2, and 'package:flutter/material.dart' only for named colors if needed. Do NOT import 'package:flame/game.dart' in component files.

When writing a test for a recorder/logger class, import and exercise the class directly rather than routing through unrelated providers. Assertions must match the actual return types of the API under test (e.g., `GameEvent.type` is a String, not an int; `events` is a List<GameEvent>, not a String).

When implementing GestureHandler fusion logic that references Flame components (MoteComponent, VectorComponent), always define `static const double tapRadius` on MoteComponent and a `setSelected(bool)` method, so GestureHandler can perform hit-testing and visual feedback without needing to reach into private state. Also ensure VectorComponent accepts named `vectorId`, `owner`, `tier`, and `position` constructor parameters so it can be spawned dynamically at a centroid position.

When a test file references a component via package import and analyze reports uri_does_not_exist or undefined_function, always verify the source file physically exists on disk by returning it in 'files' — even if its content appears correct in the task context. The most common cause of these chronic blockers is a missing source file that was never written to disk.

Never import packages (e.g. `package:flutter/foundation.dart`) in a file unless at least one symbol from that package is actually used; unused imports cause lint errors under `flutter analyze`. Prefer using built-in Dart enum `.name` getter over `.toString().split('.')` for serialisation — it is shorter, less error-prone, and avoids unnecessary string manipulation.

When implementing auto-fusion or any game-loop system that reads Riverpod state inside a Flame update(), always use ref.read() (never ref.watch()) — ref.watch() is only valid in widget build methods. Positions of units live exclusively in VectorComponent fields; the Riverpod Vector model has no position field. To find units near a world position, maintain a Map<int, VectorComponent> in the FlameGame subclass and look up components by vector id.

When implementing a time-based system (e.g. 'every N seconds'), always accumulate dt into an `_elapsed` field and reset it when the threshold is reached — never check `dt > threshold` directly, since dt is a per-frame delta (typically 0.016 s) and will never exceed multi-second thresholds. Additionally, positions are never stored in models; systems that need world positions must receive them via injected `Map<int, Vector2>` parameters updated each frame from the Flame component layer.

When astro_game.dart is locked and a task requires wiring a command into it, implement the feature as a self-contained PositionComponent (e.g. TierSelectionSystem) with TapCallbacks that can be added to the game's component tree without modifying astro_game.dart. The system must use _allDescendants() to walk the live Flame component tree rather than maintaining a separate registry, ensuring it works with dynamically added/removed units.

When a task references a class method (e.g. GameStateNotifier.fuseVectors()), check whether the class is locked in another file before creating a new file. If the class is locked, implement the logic as a standalone helper function in the target file and wire it to the notifier via ref.read(gameServiceProvider.notifier) calls. Never redeclare a locked class in a new file.

When adding tier-specific rendering to a unit component, always use a const Map keyed by tier for per-tier constants (radius, color), guard with a null-coalescing default, and place the HP arc draw call inside an `if (vector.tier == N)` guard. Import `dart:math` show pi for arc calculations, import `dart:ui` for Canvas/Color/Offset/Paint/Rect/BlendMode/MaskFilter, and always `hide Vector` from `package:flame/components.dart` when the model `Vector` class is also in scope.

When fixing test API mismatches, always verify that test thresholds and magic numbers match the actual implementation constants (e.g., FusionRules.fusionThreshold = 10, not 5). Update test expectations to match the authoritative game rules rather than inventing new thresholds — the canonical values are defined in locked rule files and the project coding rules section 'Core mechanic — never break this'.

When creating a detector/utility PositionComponent that listens for gesture events (TapCallbacks, DragCallbacks, etc.), make it self-contained: accept typed callbacks (e.g. `void Function(Vector2, int)`) and a lookup function instead of referencing internal state of other classes. Import `dart:async` for Timer, `package:flame/components.dart` for PositionComponent and Vector2, and `package:flame/events.dart` for event types. Never import `flutter/material.dart` in a Flame component unless Flutter widgets are actually being rendered.

When a task says 'remove unused imports X and Y from file F', first check the current file contents shown in the task — if those imports are already absent, the fix is to write the clean file to disk as-is (without the `// ignore_for_file` suppression comment that might mask real warnings). Never add `// ignore_for_file` to suppress unused-import warnings; instead, physically delete the unused import lines.

When writing Flame component tests in lib/ that test rendering logic without a full game loop, test the component's state fields and computed ratios directly (e.g. currentHealth / maxHealth) rather than attempting canvas rendering. Always add `// ignore_for_file: depend_on_referenced_packages` at the top of every test file inside lib/, and import sibling components with relative paths (e.g. `import 'surge_component.dart'`).

Always provide complete file contents — never truncate render(), build(), or any method body. Truncated files with dangling expressions like `..b` cause parse errors that block compilation; every returned file must be syntactically complete and self-consistent.

When adding a detection feature to PlayerPatternDetector (or any sampler class), always implement both the private backing field updated in the sampling method AND a public getter, initialise the field in _initBaseline() so it is accurate before the first timer tick, and expose a dispose() method that cancels the Timer to prevent resource leaks in tests.

When implementing star capture with an HP pool, always use `starHpProvider` (NotifierProvider<StarHpNotifier, Map<int,int>>) to track per-star HP. The capture threshold constant is `kMaxStarHp` (20). Unit power contribution uses `Vector.power` (1/3/9 for tier 1/2/3). Never invent fields like `Star.currentHealth` — positions and transient HP live outside the model layer. Always use `ref.read()` inside non-build methods (update, event handlers); `ref.watch()` is only valid inside Riverpod `build()` methods.

GestureHandler uses `Ref` (not `WidgetRef`), TapCallbacks/DragCallbacks event API (TapDownEvent, DragStartEvent, DragUpdateEvent, DragEndEvent, DragCancelEvent), and `event.localStartPosition`/`event.localDelta` for drag coordinates. Selection rectangle state (_dragStart, _dragCurrent, _isDragging) is tracked as nullable Vector2 fields and rendered in the `render` override using neon cyan with BlendMode.plus and a MaskFilter blur for glow. Never use `TapDownInfo`, `DragStartInfo`, or `info.eventPosition.game` — those are Flame <1.3 API.

When using `double.clamp()`, always pass `double` literals (e.g. `0.0`) rather than `int` literals (e.g. `0`) to avoid the return type being `num` instead of `double`, which causes type errors on typed getters. Also, if a test references a getter that does not exist on the implementation class (e.g. `totalSeconds`), add the missing getter to the implementation rather than modifying the locked test file.

When adding a callback parameter to GestureHandler (or any PositionComponent subclass that uses TapCallbacks), implement long-press detection manually with dart:async Timer in onTapDown/onTapCancel/onTapUp — Flame 1.37 has no LongPressCallbacks mixin. Cancel the timer on drag start (onDragUpdate when threshold exceeded), onTapUp, and onTapCancel to prevent false fires.

When integrating LevelGenerator into GameStateNotifier, use a dedicated `initializeSession(String sessionId)` method that calls `LevelGenerator.generateStars(sessionId)` and replaces the stars list wholesale. Never call `LevelGenerator.generate(id)` (which does not exist) inside `addStar()`; `addStar` should use a safe positional default and the docstring should direct callers to use `initializeSession` for reproducible session layouts.

When implementing long-press detection in a Flame GestureHandler (TapCallbacks + DragCallbacks), use dart:async Timer started in onTapDown and cancelled in onTapUp/onTapCancel/onDragUpdate. Store the press-start position and cancel the timer immediately in onDragUpdate (any movement cancels the long-press). Guard with a _longPressTriggered boolean so onTapUp does not also fire a tap action after the long-press fires.

For pinch-to-zoom in Flutter/Flame, wrap the GameWidget in a raw `Listener` widget and maintain a `Map<int, Offset> _pointers` to track active finger positions. On PointerDown with 2 fingers, record `_startDistance` (Euclidean distance between the two offsets) and `_startZoom` from `camera.viewfinder.zoom`. On PointerMove, recompute currentDistance and set `camera.viewfinder.zoom = (_startZoom * (currentDistance / _startDistance)).clamp(minZoom, maxZoom)`. Clear `_startDistance` when fewer than 2 pointers remain. Do NOT rely on ScaleGestureDetector or Flame scale events for this — the raw Listener approach is the only reliable method that avoids conflicts with Flame's internal gesture handling.

When adding timed visual effects to a PositionComponent, always implement them as internal state (_pulsing bool + _elapsed double) with a public trigger method (e.g. triggerProductionPulse()). External systems locate the component by walking the Flame component tree via HasGameRef and game.children, rather than storing direct references. The pulse envelope must use math.sin(t * pi) so it ramps smoothly up and back to zero over the duration.

When a task says 'update X to use Y' and the target file contains only a placeholder comment, always replace the placeholder with a real implementation that imports and applies the referenced helper (mixin, class, or function); never leave the file as a stub or add a comment saying 'no changes needed'.

When creating overlay widgets for the Flame game (tutorial, HUD, score), implement them as plain Flutter StatefulWidgets in lib/game/ using only flutter/material.dart and dart:async — never import flame/game.dart, flame/components.dart, or Riverpod unless the overlay actually needs game state. Keep Icon usage limited to built-in MaterialIcons (Icons.*) to avoid missing-asset errors, and use FadeTransition with an AnimationController for entrance/exit animation rather than AnimatedOpacity when a dispose-time cancel is required.

When integrating a modifier class (e.g. BeaconSpeedModifier) into a handler, always (1) define the modifier with a position-only constructor and a pure `multiplierFor(Vector2)` method that returns a double — never store per-unit state inside the modifier; (2) update the modifier's shared position at the top of the handler's `update()` call using data read from `ref.read(gameServiceProvider)`; (3) use `delta.clone()..normalize()` instead of `.normalize()` (which mutates and returns void in Flame); and (4) never reference a local parameter name (`surgeComponents`) inside a listener callback — always pass state through method arguments or re-read from `ref`.

When creating Flame PositionComponent subclasses, always import `package:flame/components.dart` (never `package:flame/game.dart` outside of astro_game.dart/main.dart), use `Vector2` for `size` and `position` (never `Size` or `Offset`), and never reference non-existent mixins like `Paintable`. Access `Vector2` fields with `.x`/`.y`, not `.width`/`.height`. Keep component constructors simple by accepting primitive parameters (int, double) rather than other component references when the data can be passed directly.

When a Flame Component needs to read or write Riverpod state (e.g. to dispatch move orders or read selection), pass a `Ref` into the component constructor and store it as a field; never try to access a BuildContext or WidgetRef from inside a PositionComponent/Component. Also, always import `package:flame/components.dart` in any file that uses `Vector2`, including provider files, and fix missing imports before adding new logic.

When Flame components need to interact with game state (e.g. dealing damage on arrival), never access positions or entity data through Riverpod model providers — models never store positions. Instead, split responsibilities: the Flame component handles all positional/visual logic using Vector2 fields passed at construction time, and the caller (game layer) bridges from world-space positions to model IDs before invoking notifier methods. Components that only need to spawn visual feedback (like CyanFlashComponent) require no Riverpod access at all.

When a provider file references a model type (e.g. GameOverState), always verify the model file exists at the expected path and that the import alias matches exactly. A provider in lib/game/ that duplicates one in lib/providers/ must use a distinct provider name (e.g. gameOverControllerProvider vs gameOverProvider) to avoid shadowing; the lib/game/ variant should delegate to the locked Controller class while lib/providers/ holds the canonical StateNotifier+provider pair.

When a test imports `package:astro_flux/systems/local_persistence_service.dart`, ensure that file exists as either a full implementation or a re-export of `lib/systems/persistence_service.dart` where `LocalPersistenceService` is defined; never leave this import unresolved as it causes uri_does_not_exist errors.

When implementing soft-edge visual effects (nebula, glow halos, fog), use RadialGradient.createShader() with colors fading to withAlpha(0) at the edge, assigned to Paint.shader, instead of flat-color paints with blur alone. This produces natural-looking opacity falloff without hard boundaries.

When a file uses `Vector2` from Flame, always add `import 'package:flame/components.dart';` explicitly. Never rely on transitive exports from other Flame imports to provide `Vector2`. If the file also uses `models/vector.dart`, add `hide Vector` to the Flame import to avoid the Vector/Vector2 name clash.

When initializing flame_audio (or any platform audio session), always wrap the call in an async try/catch block inside a StatefulWidget's initState so that iOS audio session permission denials or missing assets do not crash the app. Audio failure must be non-fatal: log the error with debugPrint and continue rendering the game. Never use FlameAudio.bgm.play() as a Future<T> inside FutureBuilder — bgm.play() returns void.

StreamProvider does not expose a `.notifier` getter — that pattern is only available on StateNotifierProvider and NotifierProvider. When testing StreamProvider, use a StreamController to emit values before awaiting `.future`, and manipulate data outside the provider rather than trying to mutate provider state directly via `.notifier`.

When writing end-to-end tests for game-over/victory conditions, use a plain ProviderContainer (no FlameGame or widget pump required) and assert on the Riverpod gameOverProvider state directly. The gameOverProvider's build() method must derive its state reactively from gameServiceProvider using ref.watch(), so that mutating game state (addStar/captureStar) is immediately reflected without a frame pump. Always use the exact provider name gameServiceProvider (not gameStateProvider), always pass two args to addStar(id, tier), and import models via package:astro_flux/... paths.

When a render method in a PositionComponent calls TextPaint.render or canvas.drawCircle/drawLine, always pass Vector2 (not Offset) to TextPaint.render and convert to Offset only at Canvas draw-call boundaries using position.toOffset(). If the analyzer reports argument_type_not_assignable on a canvas/text render call, check whether an Offset literal (e.g. Offset.zero) was used where Vector2 is required, and replace it with the correct Vector2 equivalent (e.g. Vector2.zero()).

When a test file references APIs that do not exist on the target class (e.g. calling `GameStateNotifier()` as a constructor when the class is `StarDecayManager`, or accessing `.decayTimer` when the getter is `.hasTimer()`), fix the test to match the actual public API of the class under test — never invent fields or constructors on the implementation class to satisfy a broken test. Also ensure implementation methods (like `tick()`) actually invoke callbacks (like `onDecay`) rather than silently no-oping, so tests that assert on side-effects can pass.

When wiring a new interaction (long-press, double-tap, etc.) that needs to attach a UI component to a game entity, implement the logic in a dedicated system class (e.g. FluxModeSystem in lib/systems/) that accepts the world root and a map of entity components. The system exposes a single public method (handleLongPress, handleDoubleTap, etc.) that AstroGame can call with the world-space position. The companion component (e.g. FluxModeSelectorComponent) must render entirely in local coordinate space (using size.x/2, size.y/2 as origin), handle its own tap dismissal via removeFromParent(), and never reference world position inside render().

Two-finger pan in Flutter+Flame: use a GestureDetector wrapping GameWidget with onScaleStart/onScaleUpdate. In onScaleUpdate, check details.pointerCount >= 2, then apply `details.focalPointDelta` to `camera.viewfinder.position` (subtract delta divided by current zoom so world-space movement matches screen-space finger movement). Do NOT use PointerDownEvent maps or custom distance calculations for pan — focalPointDelta already provides the correct incremental pan vector each frame.

When applying real-time audio volume changes via FlameAudio in settings screens, use `FlameAudio.bgm.audioPlayer.setVolume(effectiveVolume)` to update the currently playing BGM immediately. Always compute effective volume as the product of master and per-category sliders (e.g. `masterVolume * musicVolume`) and store both in a static SettingsStore so that AudioService can read `SettingsStore.effectiveMusicVolume` / `SettingsStore.effectiveSfxVolume` when starting new sounds.

When implementing a Flame component that reads Riverpod state each frame, make it a concrete PositionComponent subclass (not a mixin) that accepts a `Ref` in its constructor and calls `ref.read(provider)` inside `update()` — never `ref.watch()` outside a widget. Use `render(Canvas)` for all custom drawing, converting Vector2 positions to Offset at the draw-call boundary. Never leave render logic as stub comments.

Mixins cannot have constructor parameters or final fields initialized via constructor syntax — declare mixin state as non-final instance fields with default values instead. When a mixin needs a Flame-specific constraint (e.g. `on CameraComponent`), import the exact package that declares that class (`package:flame/camera.dart` for CameraComponent) rather than relying solely on `package:flame/components.dart`, and always import `dart:math` when using `Random`.

When writing Flutter widgets that display game entity data, never access non-existent GameState fields (e.g. `surges`, `speedMultiplier`). Always use the canonical model schema: `GameState` has `motes`, `vectors`, `stars`; `Vector` has `id`, `owner`, `tier`, `currentHealth`, `maxHealth`, `power`, `isAlive`. Derive display values (speed, label) from tier constants defined inline in the widget rather than from model fields that do not exist.

When a test file imports a model that does not exist yet, create the missing model file with the exact static members the test expects before attempting any other fix. Check all import paths in test files and ensure the corresponding source files exist with matching class names and APIs.

When writing tests for a class, use only the actual public API of that class and real Riverpod constructs (ProviderContainer as Ref). Never import or reference classes that do not exist (e.g. EnemyAI, StateNotifierProvider for Riverpod-2 notifiers). Never call private methods from tests — test them indirectly through public entry points. Always check the implementation file first and mirror its exact constructor signature and method names.

When writing integration tests for AI controllers, always access strategy state via a public getter (e.g. `counterStrategy`) rather than a private field (e.g. `_currentCounterStrategy`). If the test needs to verify an AI decision synchronously, expose a `tick(double dt)` method on the controller and call it directly rather than using `Future.delayed` — time-based delays in unit tests are fragile and slow. Provider state used in tests must be read via `container.read(provider)` and passed explicitly to class constructors; never pass a `Ref` directly to a domain class constructor.

When writing a `mixin on PositionComponent` that needs the game world size, declare a `Vector2 worldSize` field on the mixin (defaulting to `Vector2.zero()`) that the owning component sets in `onMount`. Never use `Rect.clamp`, `findGame()`, or `package:flame/game.dart` imports inside mixin files — only `package:flame/components.dart` is permitted in component/mixin files.

When writing a game-system utility that operates on Flame components, define a `mixin TrafficAvoidable on PositionComponent` (or similarly named) in the same file to declare the required interface (velocity, owner, tier, etc.). Pass `List<TrafficAvoidable>` to the system function instead of querying `game.children` directly; this avoids importing the locked `astro_game.dart`, keeps the system testable without a running FlameGame, and prevents undefined_getter/undefined_method errors caused by referencing fields that may not exist on the base PositionComponent type.

Test files under test/ must import project code via 'package:astro_flux/...' paths, never via bare filenames like 'star_capture_indicator.dart'. Bare imports only work for files in the same directory; package imports are required for cross-directory references in the test/ tree.

When writing Flame component tests that use NebulaSystem (or any system that prunes units via `isMounted`), always override `isMounted => true` in the test-only subclass so that `removeWhere` does not evict units before the system logic runs. Place such test doubles as private classes inside the test file.

When a GestureHandler (or any PositionComponent with TapCallbacks) needs to dispatch movement orders to Flame components, accept the component maps (e.g. `Map<int, MoteComponent>`, `Map<int, VectorComponent>`) as constructor parameters rather than referencing undeclared fields. Read Riverpod state with `ref.read(provider)` — never `ref.read(provider.notifier).state`. Use `DragCallbacks` mixin (not individual drag-method overrides) whenever `onDragStart/Update/End` are defined. Always import every referenced component class explicitly.

When a test file under lib/components/ uses Vector2, always import 'package:vector_math/vector_math_64.dart' (not package:flame/components.dart) because the test file is not a Flame component and does not extend PositionComponent. Also import 'package:flutter/material.dart' when Colors is referenced. Always ensure the component under test exists at lib/components/<name>.dart before writing or fixing its test.

When writing a command/query function that needs both game-state data (from Riverpod) and world-space positions (from Flame components), always (a) read state via `game.ref.read(gameServiceProvider)`, (b) compute the visible world-space rectangle from `game.camera.viewfinder.position`, `game.camera.viewfinder.zoom`, and `game.camera.viewport.size`, and (c) locate matching PositionComponents in `game.world.children` by a named ComponentKey (`ComponentKey.named('type_id')`) rather than by type-checking un-keyed components. Never reference providers that do not exist in the codebase.

When writing a test that validates a spawner/factory method returning a list of components, define a minimal concrete component class (e.g. FluxParticleComponent) in lib/components/ with the exact constructor the spawner uses, implement the spawner method to produce a deterministic-range count using Random, and ensure the test imports Vector2 explicitly from 'package:flame/components.dart' rather than relying on transitive re-exports.

When a task says 'rename field X → Y', update ALL files that reference X — both the source file defining the logic and the test file. Also remove unused imports from test files (e.g., importing a model type that is never referenced in the test body causes lint errors). The Mote model uses `state` (not `lifecycleState`) as the public field name; always use `mote.state` in application code.

When implementing time-based animation components (fade, drift, pulse), store elapsed time as a double field, update it in `update(double dt)`, derive alpha/position mathematically from the ratio `_elapsed / _duration`, and call `removeFromParent()` when elapsed >= duration. Never use Flutter's AnimationController inside a Flame PositionComponent — use the Flame update loop exclusively.

When implementing Flame PositionComponent animation subclasses, never shadow PositionComponent.position with a same-named field — use a distinct named parameter (e.g. starPosition) passed to super(position: ...). Expanding ring animations must compute radius from an eased progress value each frame, not a static offset. Text labels requiring per-frame alpha changes must create a new TextPaint per render call (or use canvas.saveLayer/restore with an alpha paint) since TextPaint style is immutable.

When adding HUD widgets that read Riverpod state (e.g. wind direction, score), implement them as Flutter CustomPainter widgets in lib/hud/ rather than Flame PositionComponents, so they compose naturally with the existing ConsumerWidget HUD overlay (lib/hud/game_hud.dart) via Stack/Positioned. Always use BlendMode.plus (not BlendMode.add), convert angles to Offset math at the canvas boundary, and never pass a Vector2 directly to canvas.drawCircle or canvas.drawLine.

When a test file specifies a constructor API (named parameters, method names) that differs from the existing implementation, rewrite the implementation to exactly match the test's expected API — do not modify the test file. Pure-Dart helper classes should not import Flame or Flutter unless the test itself imports them.

When rewriting a file that currently has broken imports and undefined references, first audit every identifier used and confirm its source file exists and exports that identifier before writing any import. For Flame component files, always import `package:flame/components.dart` (not `package:flame/game.dart`) and hide Flame's `Vector` type when also importing `models/vector.dart`. For Riverpod access, use `ref.read(provider.notifier)` to call mutating methods and `ref.read(provider)` for state reads — never call notifier methods directly on the state object.

When computing a derived display value (like score) from GameState, always derive it from the actual fields that exist on GameState (motes, vectors, stars) rather than inventing a non-existent field like `score`. Components that need Riverpod state must import `flutter_riverpod` for `Ref` and `game_state_provider.dart` for the provider; never reference undefined providers. Never add child components in a PositionComponent constructor — use `onLoad` instead.

Integration tests for game mechanics must only call methods that exist on GameStateNotifier (addMote, addVector, addStar, removeMote, removeVector, removeStar, updateStar, captureStar, damageVector, spawnSurge, clearMotes, clearVectors, clearStars, saveGame, loadGame, reset) and must only assert on fields that exist in the model schema (Star: id, tier, ownerId; Vector: id, owner, tier, currentHealth; Mote: id, lifecycleState). Never assert on fields like `currentHealth` on Star or `power` as a stored field — derive power from tier at test time. Always import `package:flutter_test/flutter_test.dart` for test/expect, and use `package:flame/components.dart` (not `package:flame/vector2.dart`) for Vector2.

When a widget needs session-scoped metrics (timePlayed, starsCaptured, etc.) that are NOT fields on GameState, declare them as explicit constructor parameters on the widget rather than accessing them via GameState. Never invent fields on GameState or any other locked model — always check the 'Current model schema' section before referencing a field.

When implementing dashed/animated lines on a Flame Canvas, do NOT use Paint.dashPattern or Paint.dashOffset (those properties do not exist in dart:ui). Instead, use Path.computeMetrics() + PathMetric.extractPath() to manually slice the path into dash segments, advancing a _dashOffset accumulator each update() tick. Wrap _dashOffset modulo the cycle length (_dashLength + _gapLength) to keep it bounded.

When writing tests for AI evaluator classes that score game entities, always use only real model fields from the locked schema (Star: id, tier, ownerId, productionRate getter). Never reference fields that do not exist on the model (e.g. currentHealth, position on Star). Star positions must be injected via a Map<int, Vector2> parameter on the evaluator — never stored on the model. Use ProviderContainer with overrideWith to supply a fresh GameStateNotifier(LocalPersistenceService()) and call only the notifier methods listed in the project rules (addStar, captureStar, updateStar, etc.).

When a test file is listed as locked but contains compile errors that must be fixed, the test file itself must be rewritten to fix the errors (e.g., removing illegal `const` from non-const constructors), since PositionComponent subclasses cannot be const-constructed. The locked-file restriction applies to production source files; test files named `*_test.dart` under `lib/components/` may be rewritten when the task explicitly targets them for error fixes.

When defining Color constants in enum extensions, always use the full 8-digit hex form 0xFFRRGGBB (with FF alpha prefix). Omitting or misplacing the alpha byte produces incorrect Color values (e.g. 0xFFDD00 is parsed as fully-transparent dark yellow, not cyan). Verify every Color literal has exactly 8 hex digits after '0x'.

When a test imports a component that has been emptied/deprecated, restore the real implementation in the component file rather than modifying the test. Ensure every constructor parameter referenced in any test is present in the actual class — never leave component files as empty stubs if tests depend on them.

When writing tests for AI or utility classes, import only the class under test and its direct dependencies (e.g. flame/components.dart for Vector2). Never import gameServiceProvider or model position fields in unit tests for pure-logic classes — models have no position field per the schema, and provider state is not needed when the class under test accepts plain Vector2 arguments.

When implementing double-tap detection in Flame 1.37 (which lacks DoubleTapCallbacks), use a manual timestamp-and-ID approach inside onTapDown: record the tapped entity ID and DateTime on first tap, and on second tap within a threshold (e.g. 300ms) on the same entity trigger the double-tap handler. Never import or reference DoubleTapCallbacks — it does not exist in this Flame version.

When adding display fields to an overlay widget that depends on a provider-backed state model, always (1) add the new field to the state data class with a sensible default so existing callers compile unchanged, (2) add a corresponding parameter to the notifier's trigger method, and (3) render the field in the widget — all three changes in the same task to keep state, provider, and UI in sync and prevent undefined_getter errors.

When a test file references identifiers that do not exist (e.g., `decayTimer`, `GameStateNotifier` used as a function), rewrite the test to use only the real public API of the class under test. Never reference fields or constructors that are not declared in the implementation file; check the implementation first and align the test to it.

When a test reports 'undefined function' for a model constructor (e.g. Star, Mote), the root cause is almost always a missing or mismatched model file in lib/models/. Before touching the test file, verify the model class exists with a constructor whose named parameters exactly match the test's usage — including optional fields like `tier: 0` or `ownerId: ''`. Create or correct the model file first, then re-run analysis.

When a PositionComponent subclass declares a field named `position`, it shadows the inherited `PositionComponent.position` field and causes a compile error. Never declare a field named `position` (or `size`, `anchor`, `scale`) inside a PositionComponent subclass — use the constructor's `super` call or the inherited field directly instead.

When creating a PositionComponent that covers the full screen (e.g. a flash overlay), accept a `Vector2 screenSize` constructor parameter and set `size: screenSize` — never hard-code a size or query a global screen size from inside a component. The component's position should be Vector2.zero() so it fills from the top-left corner.

For pinch-to-zoom implemented via raw Listener pointer events: record `_pinchStartZoom = _game.camera.viewfinder.zoom` when the second finger goes down, then on each pointer move compute `newZoom = (_pinchStartZoom * (currentDistance / startDistance)).clamp(0.35, 2.0)` and assign directly to `_game.camera.viewfinder.zoom`. Never pass a relative scale to a game method that applies it multiplicatively each frame — that causes exponential zoom drift.

When a class is referenced by string key (e.g. overlayKey) or by name in comments/docs inside other files, always define a matching static const overlayKey in the widget class itself, and ensure the corresponding model files (e.g. GameOverReason enum) exist before referencing them — missing models are a top source of undefined_identifier errors in this project.

When a test file references a getter (e.g. `isActive`) that maps to a private field (e.g. `_isActive`) in a component, always add a public getter in the component class that simply returns the private field. Never leave private state inaccessible to tests; expose it via a read-only public getter so that `flutter analyze` passes without modifying the test file.

When rewriting a file that references non-existent classes (e.g. MoteComponent, SurgeComponent, Collidable) or uses stale Flame collision APIs, replace the entire file with a clean implementation that uses only verified project APIs. For arrival/investment logic use a plain Dart class with explicit `handleXArrival(Vector unit, Star star, String owner)` methods rather than Flame collision callbacks, since the collision system depends on component wiring that may not exist. Always access GameStateNotifier via `ref.read(gameServiceProvider.notifier)` — never via `ref.read(gameServiceProvider).methodName()`.

When a PositionComponent needs both tap and drag handling, add both TapCallbacks and DragCallbacks as mixins on the same class. Always implement onDragCancel alongside onDragStart/Update/End to clean up drag state. Store drag start/current as nullable Vector2 fields and null them out in onDragEnd/onDragCancel. Render the selection rectangle in the component's render() override using BlendMode.plus (never BlendMode.add) with a MaskFilter.blur glow; always check for null before drawing. Use dart:ui imports for Color, Canvas, Paint, Rect, Offset, MaskFilter, and BlendMode.

When a Flame PositionComponent is destroyed, spawn any death-effect component (e.g. UnitShatterComponent) inside onRemove() by calling parent?.add(...) with absolutePosition.clone() as the spawn position, and guard against double-spawning by checking parent != null. Never call removeFromParent() from within onRemove() — Flame calls onRemove() as part of the removal cycle, so triggering removal again causes infinite recursion. A die() helper method should call _spawnShatter() then removeFromParent(); onRemove() should only fire the effect when the component is removed via other code paths (parent != null guard prevents the double-spawn).

When creating a Flame PositionComponent that handles taps, always use `TapCallbacks` mixin (not `Tappable`), pass `Vector2` (never `Offset`) to `super(position:)`, implement real rendering logic in `render(Canvas canvas)` using `canvas.drawCircle(Offset(...), radius, paint)` with `dart:ui` imported, and never store `BuildContext` as a field in a Flame component.

When fixing test files for 'missing param' or 'missing member' errors, always cross-check the exact constructor signatures and field names in the model files before editing. If a test references a constructor parameter that doesn't exist on the model, update the test to match the actual model API (never invent new model fields). Also ensure test files import all service classes they exercise, and add concrete test cases that actually call service methods to validate the service compiles and works correctly.

When writing tests for classes that use real `Timer` or `dart:async` timers, always control time with `fakeAsync` from `package:fake_async/fake_async.dart` (a transitive dep of flutter_test). Never rely on `await Future.delayed(...)` in unit tests — real delays make tests slow and flaky. Use `fake.elapse(Duration(...))` to advance the fake clock, and call any simulation helpers (e.g. `tick()`) before each `elapse` call so the system-under-test sees the right state when the timer fires.

When writing stubs or placeholders that reference model classes, always check the 'Current model schema' section to confirm which fields actually exist before writing field accesses. Star has no 'position', 'currentHealth', or 'maxHealth' — positions are a Flame/component concern, never stored in models. Also ensure all Dart imports (dart:math, etc.) are present and never reference non-existent static members like AstroGame.screenSize.

When creating a star/glow component that renders circular halos, always draw actual circles with canvas.drawCircle(Offset, radius, Paint) using Offset.zero as center (since PositionComponent already translates the canvas). Never draw paths/polygons for a 'ring' effect. Import dart:ui for Canvas, Paint, Offset, MaskFilter, BlendMode, and dart:math for sin/pi. Use Color.fromARGB instead of deprecated withOpacity/withAlpha chains for clarity.

StateNotifier state must never be mutated in-place. Always replace the entire state object with an immutable copy: `state = Map<int, double>.from(state)..[key] = value;`. Direct mutation like `state[key] = value` silently fails to notify listeners and violates Riverpod's immutability contract.

Before attempting to remove unused imports from a file, verify that those imports actually exist in the current file contents. If the file already lacks the named imports, it is already clean — return the file unchanged and mark the task done without modification.

When writing Riverpod-based tests, always use a plain `ProviderContainer()` (with `addTearDown(container.dispose)`) to host notifiers under test. Never use `ProviderScope.runAppBuilder`, `ProviderScope.containerOf`, or Flutter widget infrastructure in pure-logic unit tests. Seed state via the notifier's own provider methods (e.g. `container.read(gameServiceProvider.notifier).addStar(...)`) rather than constructing notifiers directly.

When GameState is defined in lib/models/game_state.dart, it must NOT also define an enum with the same name. Use a distinct name (e.g. GameStatus) for any status enum. The GameState class constructor must have default values for all list fields (e.g. `this.motes = const []`) so that `const GameState()` compiles without arguments, and a copyWith method must be present. Files in lib/game/ that use GameStateNotifier extension methods must declare a Provider that returns the notifier from gameServiceProvider rather than a separate StateNotifier, and the extension must be on GameStateNotifier directly.

When creating a model/event class, always include: (1) the @immutable annotation from flutter/foundation.dart when all fields are final, (2) proper == operator and hashCode overrides using all fields, and (3) a toString() override — this makes the class fully usable in collections, debugging, and equality checks without requiring future fixes.

Never call `.toVector2()` on a Flame `Vector2` (it does not exist); `Vector2` is already the correct type — use `.clone()` to get a copy. Also avoid using `Colors.*` constants from flutter/material.dart in files that only import `dart:ui`; instead use `const Color(0xAARRGGBB)` literals or add the flutter/material.dart import explicitly.

Integration tests for Flame components must use `FlameGame` directly (not widget finders or GameStateNotifier): create a `FlameGame` instance, call `await game.onLoad()`, add components with `game.add(...)`, call `game.update(dt)` to pump a tick, then assert using `game.children.whereType<T>()`. Never use `find.byType()` (Flutter widget finder) or `container.read(notifier).state` mutations in Flame component integration tests. Also, `PositionComponent` has no `.velocity` or `.color` fields — store those in subclass fields only.

When creating short-lived particle components, always extend PositionComponent directly (not PositionComponent with HasGameRef unless game access is needed), use `removeFromParent()` when lifetime expires, compute random values in static helper methods (not inline field initializers that reference instance fields), and always use `BlendMode.plus` with `import 'dart:ui'` for neon glow effects.

When a file defines a class that is also declared via a `part` directive in another file, this causes a duplicate class error. Never use `part`/`part of` for model or zone classes — each class must live in exactly one file, and other files must import it normally. Additionally, always ensure `NebulaZone` uses a `center` field (not `position`) to match the `NebulaComponent` render code that references `zone.center`.

When removing debug UI elements (FloatingActionButton zoom buttons, debug overlays, etc.), also remove their corresponding game class methods (debugZoomIn/debugZoomOut) in the same task. Never leave orphaned debug methods in game classes after their UI triggers are removed.

When implementing star HP accumulation in AstroGame, use an internal `Map<int, int> _starAccumulatedPower` to track cumulative power per star id, since the locked Star model has no `currentHealth` field. Use `ref.read(gameServiceProvider)` (not `ref.watch`) inside non-build methods like `update()` and event handlers to avoid Riverpod misuse errors.

When writing tests for tap/gesture detectors, avoid Flame's TapDownEvent/DragStartEvent constructors in unit tests — their internal constructors are not stable for direct instantiation. Instead, extract the detection logic into a pure-Dart class (no Flame imports) that accepts a callback and exposes a `recordTap()` method; the Flame component then simply calls `recordTap()` from its `onTapDown` override. This keeps tests fast, hermetic, and free of Flame widget-tree dependencies.

Never define a Dart class with the same name as an imported class from a dependency (e.g., `class PositionComponent extends PositionComponent` is a recursive self-reference and compile error). If a compatibility shim file is no longer needed, replace its body with a comment explaining why it is empty rather than redefining existing framework classes.

When creating a hit-feedback flash component, always create it as a standalone PositionComponent (e.g. CyanFlashComponent) that tracks its own elapsed/lifetime, removes itself via removeFromParent() when expired, and renders with BlendMode.plus and a MaskFilter.blur. Never store positions in model objects — derive world position from the component's own position field. The flash component must be added via parent?.add() at the hit site, not via a manager or provider.

When a task requests defining a model class and the file already exists with all required attributes, constructors (const, factory fromJson), toJson, copyWith, toString, ==, and hashCode implemented correctly, mark the task as complete without modification — do not re-implement or restructure an already-correct file.

When using a `Map<int, bool>` for unlock state, always check membership with `map[key] == true` (not `map[key]!` or `!map[key]`) because the value is nullable (`bool?`); `!null` throws at runtime and `map.containsKey` + separate access is verbose. Also ensure level indices are 1-based (use `index + 1` in GridView.builder) to avoid off-by-one bugs between display labels and unlock keys.

When adding or modifying public API on a component, ensure private getters (e.g. `_color`) that are referenced in tests are renamed to public getters (e.g. `neonColor`), and keep magic constants (like default radius) in sync between implementation and test expectations — never leave a private getter that a test needs to access, and always verify numeric constants match across both files before committing.

Test files in lib/ must only import packages that are actually used in the file body. Remove unused imports (flutter_riverpod, game_state_provider, etc.) to avoid undefined_identifier and ambiguous_import errors. Every test must contain at least one expect() call so the test runner does not flag it as trivially empty.

When writing integration/unit tests for fusion effects, do NOT attempt to construct AstroGame directly in a test (it is locked and has complex dependencies). Instead: (1) test ImplosionEffectComponent by instantiating it directly and calling update(); (2) test ScreenShakeController by constructing it with a no-op applyOffset callback and asserting isShaking before/after startShake(); (3) test the Riverpod screenShakeServiceProvider via a plain ProviderContainer. Always add an isShaking getter to ScreenShakeController that returns true iff startShake has been called and the elapsed time has not yet exceeded the duration.

When a test file imports a library path that does not yet exist (e.g. `package:astro_flux/components/sparkle_renderer.dart`), always create the corresponding source file with a real, compilable implementation before attempting to fix the test itself. A missing source file causes every downstream analyzer error in the test; creating the file resolves all of them at once.

Test files under test/ must always use package:astro_flux/... imports (not relative ../imports) for any file in lib/. Relative imports from test/ to lib/ will always fail because the test/ directory is not inside lib/. Only test files physically inside lib/ may use relative imports.

When a task asks you to implement an AI behavior that references an enum variant not yet in its enum (e.g. CounterStrategy.defendWithPulsar), always update the enum in the same output as the new behavior file — never leave an undefined enum value that will cause a compile error. Also ensure any model or state file referenced by the behavior (e.g. star_flux_state.dart) is fully implemented (not a stub) before the behavior file imports it.

When implementing gesture handlers that interact with Flame components, always check that component APIs (e.g., `tapRadius`, `setSelected`, `moveTo`, `moteId`, `vectorId`) exist on the referenced component classes before calling them. If a component does not yet expose a needed method or constant, add it in the same task. Never leave a gesture handler method body incomplete or truncated — the anti-stub rule requires every method to contain working Dart code with an observable result.

When a test file expects a class with a specific constructor signature and instance methods, always implement the production code as a proper Dart class (not a free-standing static function or invalid top-level code). Verify the production file compiles as valid Dart by ensuring all code is inside class/function bodies before writing the test.

When a task says 'implement X in Y.methodName()', add the method directly to the class in the canonical provider/notifier file (e.g., GameStateNotifier in lib/models/game_state_provider.dart), not as a standalone top-level function in a separate file. Standalone helper functions are acceptable as internal utilities, but the public API method must exist on the notifier class so callers can use `ref.read(provider.notifier).methodName()`.

When writing tests for mixins or utility classes, test the actual class/mixin directly by creating a minimal concrete subclass in the test file — never introduce non-existent helper classes (like AIThreatEvaluator) or attempt to manipulate Riverpod notifier state directly. Keep test imports minimal: only import what is actually used, and avoid importing provider packages when the test does not require state management.

When creating a Flame PositionComponent, never use `part`/`part of` code-generation directives, `@Riverpod()` annotations, non-existent mixins (HasRenderPriority, Pausable), or non-existent constants (RenderPriority.overlay). Always extend PositionComponent (not bare Component) when you need `position` and `size` fields, import `dart:math` for `pi`/`cos`/`sin`, and import `dart:ui` for `Canvas`, `Paint`, `Offset`, `Color`, `BlendMode`, and `MaskFilter`.

When defining compile-time or near-const data structures that use Flame's Vector2 (which is not const-constructible), omit the `const` keyword on the constructor and ensure the file imports `package:flame/components.dart` so Vector2 is in scope. Never use `const LevelConfig(...)` if any field type (like Vector2) cannot be constructed as a const.

When importing a file with a `hide` clause, only hide symbols that are actually exported by that file. A `hide` clause referencing a non-existent symbol causes a compile error (uri_does_not_exist or invocation_of_non_function). Before adding `hide SomeClass` to an import, verify that `SomeClass` is actually declared/exported in the target file.

When a mixin on PositionComponent needs to act as a standalone child component added to FlameGame, convert it to a concrete PositionComponent subclass with a final Ref field set via constructor — mixins cannot hold final instance fields and cannot be added directly as children. Use ref.read() (not ref.watch()) inside update() to poll Riverpod state in the game loop; ref.watch() is only valid inside widget build methods.

When implementing tap-to-deselect in AstroGame, use a `unitPositions` registry (Map<int, Vector2>) that components update each frame via `registerUnitPosition`/`unregisterUnit`. `_handleSingleTap` iterates this registry; if no entry is within 30 px of the tap, call `ref.read(selectionProvider.notifier).clearSelection()`. Never access `position` on a model class — positions live only in Flame components.

When a file imports both `dart:ui` and any Flutter package (e.g. `flutter/painting.dart`, `flutter/material.dart`), always hide conflicting names from `dart:ui` that are re-exported by Flutter: `import 'dart:ui' hide TextDirection;`. In particular, `dart:ui` and `flutter/painting.dart` both export `TextDirection`, causing ambiguity errors — always hide it from `dart:ui` when using Flutter's `TextPainter`/`TextSpan` APIs.

When writing Riverpod integration tests that do not involve Flutter widgets, use ProviderContainer directly with plain dart:test (via flutter_test) — no FlameGame, no pumpWidget. Pass `container.read` as the `Ref` argument to pure-Dart system classes so they can read providers without a running Flutter app. Always resolve enum conflicts between files by consolidating to a single canonical definition and importing it everywhere rather than re-declaring the same enum in multiple files.

When a test file imports a module that does not yet exist, create the missing module(s) with the exact API the test expects (class names, method signatures, property names, and return types) before running analysis. Never leave a test file with unresolved import URIs or undefined identifiers.

When implementing a sampling/detection class that reads game state periodically, always guard against division-by-zero by checking the denominator before dividing, use a proper rolling List window (add to end, removeAt(0) when over capacity) rather than resetting accumulators, and detect state changes by diffing the current snapshot against the previous snapshot stored as a field — never rely on external callbacks for events that can be derived from successive state samples.

When a Notifier needs to react to selection changes (lasso confirmed), add a dedicated `onSelectionConfirmed` method that accepts the confirmed unit IDs and their current positions, looks up internal `_lastKnownTargets` per unit, and updates state — do NOT use a Riverpod `ref.listen` inside build() unless the notifier is an `AsyncNotifier`; instead expose imperative methods that game systems call directly after confirming the selection.

When creating a new Riverpod notifier, always use Riverpod 2.x API: extend `Notifier<State>` (not `StateNotifier<State>`), implement `build()` returning the initial state, and declare a `NotifierProvider` (not `StateNotifierProvider`). For cross-provider reads inside a notifier, use `ref.read(otherProvider)` inside the method body — never store provider refs as instance fields. Always create a companion `Provider` (e.g. `vectorProvider`) that reads from `gameServiceProvider` so other notifiers can override it in tests via `ProviderContainer(overrides: [...])`.

When writing tests for a new system class (e.g. PulsarFlareSystem), create the system implementation first with the exact constructor signature and public API that the test exercises, use only dt-based simulation (never real Future.delayed for game-loop timing), and avoid importing providers/notifiers unless the system truly requires them — keep unit tests free of Riverpod and Flame game lifecycle dependencies.

When adding HUD elements to `lib/hud/game_hud.dart`, always implement them as Flutter widgets (not Flame PositionComponents) since GameHud is a ConsumerWidget. Pair each new HUD visual with a Riverpod Notifier provider in `lib/providers/` for its state, and use CustomPainter for any neon/glow rendering inside the widget tree. Never import `package:flame/components.dart` from HUD widget files.

When adding a configuration parameter to a FlameGame subclass (e.g. LevelConfig), define it in its own file under lib/game/, make it an optional named parameter with a documented default, and call super.onLoad() first in the override so that `size` is valid before any layout calculations. Never call ref.watch() inside update() or onLoad() — use ref.read() for one-shot reads during game-loop methods.

When implementing ghost-path or waypoint rendering, never use Paint.dashPattern (it does not exist in Flutter's dart:ui Paint API). Instead, implement dash patterns manually by walking the path length with alternating draw/skip segments using moveTo/lineTo.
