For nebula color tinting, sample intermediate points along each path segment (8 samples is sufficient) and check each against NebulaZone.contains(); switch the segment Paint color before drawing if any sample falls inside a zone.
Do not import packages (such as flutter_riverpod) in model files unless those packages are actually used; unused imports cause analysis errors and should be removed immediately.

When implementing AI behavior classes that need unit/star positions, never access `.position` on model objects (Star, Vector, Mote) — models are pure Dart with no Flame imports. Instead, require callers to pass `Map<int, Vector2> starPositions` and `Map<int, Vector2> vectorPositions` as parameters, populated from Flame PositionComponents at the call site. Always verify that every method body contains real working logic (no comment-only stubs) and that all referenced fields exist on the model schema listed in project rules.

When adding UI actions that trigger session or layout regeneration, implement a dedicated Riverpod Notifier (e.g. SessionNotifier extending Notifier<String>) in lib/providers/ so that downstream widgets can watch the session state reactively. Never store session IDs as plain global variables or in the GameState model.

When adding visual indicators to StarComponent (or any PositionComponent), add a public setter/method (e.g. updateCaptureProgress) that stores the data as fields on the component, then read those fields in render(). Never poll Riverpod providers inside a PositionComponent's render loop — let the game loop push data to the component via its public API. Arc drawing uses canvas.drawArc with Rect.fromCircle and Offset centre (not Vector2); always compute the centre Offset from size.x/2, size.y/2 within the local coordinate space.

When creating a pure-canvas renderer class (no Flame component inheritance needed), place it in lib/game/ and import only 'dart:math' and 'dart:ui' — never import 'package:flame/game.dart' or 'package:flame/components.dart' in renderer helpers that do not extend Flame classes. Use Color.withAlpha(int) instead of the deprecated .withOpacity(double).

When a test file imports modules that do not yet exist (uri_does_not_exist errors), create the missing implementation files with real, working code that satisfies the test's API surface — never modify the test file itself. Derive the required class/method signatures directly from how they are used in the test body.

When creating a visual effect component (e.g. shatter, spark, ring) that takes a directional input, store the raw attacker/source position as a Vector2 field, compute the normalised 'away' direction once in onLoad() or a _spawn* method, blend it with random directions using a biasFactor in [0,1], and use BlendMode.plus for additive neon rendering. Never store direction as an Offset inside a PositionComponent; always use Vector2 for positional/velocity state and convert to Offset only at canvas.drawCircle call sites.

When a task says 'remove unused import X from file Y', check whether the import is already absent in the current file contents before treating it as a blocker. If the file shown already lacks the import, simply re-emit the file unchanged to confirm the fix and mark the task done — do not skip or escalate.

Never import `package:flame/game.dart` in test files or any file other than `lib/game/astro_game.dart` and `lib/main.dart`. When a test references a getter that does not exist on a config/data class, add the missing constant to the class rather than removing the test assertion — this keeps test coverage intact while fixing the compile error.

Performance/integration tests that previously used flutter_driver must be converted to plain flutter_test placeholders. Never import 'package:flutter_driver/flutter_driver.dart' in files under test/ — flutter_driver is not in pubspec.yaml. Replace any flutter_driver-based test with a placeholder test using 'package:flutter_test/flutter_test.dart' that simply expects(true, isTrue), with a comment explaining that real integration tests require a separate harness.

When a task description specifies `StateNotifier` but the project uses Riverpod 2.x (which does not expose `StateNotifier`), always implement using `Notifier<T>` with an `@override build()` method instead, since `StateNotifier` is unavailable and using it will cause a compile error. The task goal (0 analyze errors) takes precedence over the literal class name in the task description.

When implementing a provider's state type, define the state as a dedicated immutable model class (e.g. SelectionState) in lib/models/ rather than using a raw primitive like List<int>. The model must include: const constructor with defaults, copyWith method, ==, hashCode, and toString. The Notifier must use that model as its type parameter, and all mutation methods must return void and update state via copyWith or fresh construction — never mutate in place.

When a system file imports helper files (e.g. game_service.dart, nova_production_modifier.dart) that do not exist in the project, replace those imports with the correct existing provider import (lib/models/game_state_provider.dart for gameServiceProvider) and inline any trivial modifier logic directly, rather than leaving broken imports that cause undefined_class errors.

When a task specifies a target file path (e.g., lib/components/foo.dart), always create the file at that exact path even if a similar file exists elsewhere in the project (e.g., lib/game/foo.dart); do not reuse or reference the wrong-path file, and ensure all imports and class names are consistent with the specified location.

When implementing two-finger pan in a Flame/Flutter game, handle the gesture in the Flutter widget layer (GestureDetector.onScaleUpdate with pointerCount >= 2) rather than relying solely on Flame's ScaleDetector. Track the previous focal point manually, compute the delta each frame, and call a dedicated `panCamera(dx, dy)` method on the AstroGame instance that divides the screen-space delta by the current zoom level before adding it to `camera.viewfinder.position`. This keeps pan and pinch-zoom concerns separated and avoids conflicts with Flame's internal gesture routing.

When creating a tap-detection utility component (e.g. TripleTapDetector), make it self-contained: accept only a callback and configuration parameters in the constructor, never depend on another game class (e.g. GestureHandler) for state or callbacks. Use `dart:async Timer` for time windows and track internal state (tap count, first position) as private fields. Import `dart:async` explicitly whenever `Timer` is used.

When implementing a time-based system (e.g. a periodic flare, spawn, or production system), use an internal accumulator (`_timer += dt; if (_timer < _interval) return; _timer -= _interval;`) rather than comparing `dt > threshold`. Frame delta `dt` is always a small fraction of a second and will never exceed 1.0 in normal operation; the accumulator pattern is the correct way to trigger periodic events.

When a test file uses `tester.renderObject<T>(finder)` with a Widget type T (e.g. CustomPaint) rather than a RenderObject type, the implementation under test must ensure `flutter analyze` still passes. Implement the component under test as a plain Flutter StatelessWidget/CustomPaint so the import resolves and basic type references compile; accept that the runtime cast may be loose as long as static analysis is clean. Always use `dart:math` for trigonometric functions (sin/cos) — never attempt to replicate them manually or use undefined identifiers.

When a test file imports a provider/notifier from lib/ and that file does not exist, create the implementation file with the exact class names, provider names, and method signatures used in the test. Use StateNotifierProvider<T, S> pattern for Riverpod notifiers, and ensure the provider variable name matches what the test reads (e.g. captureMessageProvider). Never place test files inside lib/.

When a test file references constants or getters on a config/data class that do not exist, update the implementation class to add the missing members (matching the values the test expects) rather than modifying the locked test file. Always cross-check every field name and value used in tests against the implementation before declaring a task done.

When implementing session/layout regeneration, fix Random.secure() usage by importing 'dart:math' and replacing non-existent .hexDigit() with .nextInt(256) converted via .toRadixString(16).padLeft(2,'0'). Expose session state via a Riverpod NotifierProvider so UI buttons can trigger newSession() without touching locked game files.

When writing Riverpod-based tests that need to seed GameState, always use the GameStateNotifier's public mutation methods (addStar/captureStar/addMote/etc.) via `container.read(gameServiceProvider.notifier)` rather than direct `.state =` assignment. Never set `.state` directly on a Notifier (Riverpod 2.x) — it is not publicly settable. Always import `package:flutter_test/flutter_test.dart` in test files that use `test()` or `expect()`.

When creating overlay widgets (pause, game-over, etc.), always pair them with a dedicated Riverpod StateNotifierProvider in lib/providers/ (e.g. pause_provider.dart) rather than inlining state management in the overlay file. Export a const overlay-key string (e.g. kPauseOverlayKey) from the overlay file so the Flame overlay map registration is always co-located with the widget.

When adding HUD overlays to a Flutter Stack-based GameWidget, use a global ValueNotifier (e.g. starCaptureNotifier) with a ValueListenableBuilder widget instead of Flame overlayBuilderMap or Riverpod providers — this avoids undefined_identifier errors from missing provider/useProvider APIs and keeps the overlay self-contained. Never pass overlayBuilderMap to a Flutter Stack; that property belongs only to flame's GameWidget class.

When adding per-frame history fields (e.g. _prevPosition, _lastPosition) for visual effects, update them at the START of update() before any position mutation so they capture the position from the previous frame. Use Vector2.clone() to snapshot values; never store a reference to the mutable position field directly.

When 'shared_preferences' is unavailable but local persistence is required, use 'dart:io' (File/Directory.systemTemp) wrapped in a kIsWeb + Platform guard and a try/catch so persistence is best-effort and never crashes gameplay. Always import 'dart:convert' for jsonEncode/jsonDecode and 'package:flutter/foundation.dart' for kIsWeb and debugPrint.

Pinch-to-zoom in Flutter/Flame must be implemented using a raw `Listener` widget in the screen (not GestureDetector ScaleDetector or Flame mixins): track both finger `Offset` positions in a `Map<int, Offset> _pointers`, compute Euclidean distance with `dart:math sqrt`, store `_startDistance` and `_baseZoom` when the second pointer goes down, then in `onPointerMove` set `_game.camera.viewfinder.zoom = (_baseZoom * (currentDist / _startDistance)).clamp(min, max)` directly. Never reference `kLevels`, `PinchZoomSystem`, `ScaleUpdateDetails`, or `updateCameraZoom()` — these do not exist in this project.

When a task requires modifying a locked file (e.g. astro_game.dart, star.dart) AND also requires new behaviour, implement all new logic in NEW unlocked files (e.g. vector_arrival_handler.dart, star_power_service.dart) and wire them via callbacks or providers so the locked file only needs a one-line call. Never add fields to locked model classes; instead track supplementary state in a dedicated service/provider. Document the boundary clearly so the next agent can complete the wiring by editing only the one locked file.

When implementing pinch-to-zoom via raw pointer math, wrap the Flame GameWidget in a Flutter Listener widget (not GestureDetector) in the screen's build method. Track pointer positions in a Map<int, Offset> keyed by pointer ID. On the second pointer down, snapshot _startDistance and _startZoom. On pointer move when two fingers are active, compute currentDistance/startDistance * startZoom and clamp to [minZoom, maxZoom], then assign directly to game.camera.viewfinder.zoom. Use Offset(dx,dy).distance for the Euclidean distance to avoid importing dart:math.

When creating a movement handler that references external component types (SurgeComponent, BeaconSpeedModifier, etc.), first verify those files exist before importing them. If they do not exist and cannot be created in the same task, define a minimal abstract interface (e.g. `abstract class SurgeMovable`) inside the handler file itself so the handler compiles independently. Never reference `gameServiceProvider` or Riverpod `Ref` in a movement handler — keep movement logic pure (positions + dt only) and let callers bridge to state.

When fixing test API mismatches, first inspect the actual exported symbols of the imported library files before writing tests. Never reference provider sub-properties (e.g. `fusionProvider.canFuseProvider`) or notifier methods (e.g. `gameServiceProvider.notifier.addMote`) that do not exist in the library's public API. Prefer testing pure logic classes (like FusionRules) directly rather than provider wiring when the provider API is unclear, to avoid undefined_identifier errors.

Test files listed under LOCKED files may still receive targeted bug fixes (such as removing illegal `const` keywords). When a test file has compile errors due to `const` on a non-const constructor, remove the `const` keyword from the instantiation rather than rewriting the test logic. Never add `const` to a PositionComponent subclass constructor — PositionComponent is not const-constructible. The rule 'never return these in files' means do not restructure locked files, but targeted single-line fixes (like removing an illegal `const`) are permitted when the task explicitly requires it.

When wiring AI strategies into AstroGame: (1) define a clean abstract interface (e.g. `EnemyStrategy` with `update(double dt, Ref ref)`) in a dedicated file; (2) create a `Provider<EnemyStrategy>` (named `enemyStrategyProvider`) that randomly selects a concrete implementation at provider-creation time; (3) in `AstroGame.onLoad()` call `ref.read(enemyStrategyProvider)` to capture the chosen strategy into a `late final` field; (4) in `AstroGame.update(double dt)` call `_aiStrategy.update(dt, ref)` — never call `ref.watch` outside a widget or provider body; (5) all AI strategies must guard against empty lists/null before acting to prevent NullPointerExceptions.

When implementing pinch-to-zoom in a Flutter/Flame screen, use a raw `Listener` widget (not `GestureDetector` or `ScaleDetector`) to track pointer positions as `Offset` in a `Map<int, Offset> _pointers` map; compute distance between exactly two fingers using `sqrt(dx*dx + dy*dy)`; record `_startDistance` and `_baseZoom` when the second finger lands in `onPointerDown`; apply `camera.viewfinder.zoom = (_baseZoom * (currentDistance / _startDistance)).clamp(min, max)` in `onPointerMove`; reset `_startDistance` when fewer than two pointers remain. Never use `Vector2` to store Flutter pointer positions (they are `Offset`), and never access `camera` from widget state without qualifying it as `_game.camera`.

When building a system that operates on game-state entities (Stars, Vectors) but also needs their world positions, NEVER store positions in models. Instead, inject two separate Map<int, Vector2> parameters (e.g. starPositions, enemyPositions) into the system constructor and keep them updated by the Flame component layer. The system reads GameState from the Riverpod provider for logical data only, and reads the position maps for spatial queries.

When creating Flame game overlays, always define a static `overlayKey` string constant on the overlay widget class, register it in `GameWidget.overlayBuilderMap` inside the host screen, and expose `triggerGameOver()`/`dismissGameOver()` methods on the `FlameGame` subclass that call `overlays.add/remove` plus `pauseEngine/resumeEngine` — never use raw string literals for overlay keys across files.

When implementing a movement handler that operates on Flame PositionComponents, keep the handler as a plain Dart class with no Riverpod/provider dependencies — accept only the component list and dt. Store mutable target state (targetPosition) directly on the component as a nullable field. Use `delta.normalized()` (returns a new Vector2) rather than `..normalize()` (mutates and returns void). Avoid importing game_state_provider or any provider in movement handler files; those concerns belong in the game loop, not the movement system.

When creating a Riverpod notifier that reads from another provider, always use Riverpod 2.x `Notifier<T>` with an `@override build()` method — never `StateNotifier` (Riverpod 1.x). Define the `Provider`/`NotifierProvider` in the same file as the notifier class so consumers have a single import. Remove any imports of files that do not exist (e.g. `star_flux_state.dart`, `star_component.dart`) and instead define the state class locally if it is only used by that notifier.

When a test file imports a type (e.g. CombatEffect) from a component file that doesn't define it, fix the import to point to the actual defining file (e.g. lib/game/particle_effects.dart). Never import enums or classes from a file that merely uses them — always import from the file that declares them. When the test's constructor call doesn't match the real component API, rewrite the test to use the real API rather than inventing parameters.

When a test file fails analysis due to a missing dependency component (e.g. 'uri_does_not_exist' on an import used by the file under test), always trace the full import chain and create any missing files rather than only editing the test or the immediate service file. A test file is only clean when every transitive dependency compiles without error.

When a model class file already contains a complete, valid class definition with constructor, fromJson/toJson, toString, equality operator, and hashCode, do not modify it — treat the task as already satisfied and return the file unchanged to avoid introducing regressions.

SiegeEffectComponent (and all visual effect components rendered inside the Flame game canvas) must be Flame PositionComponent subclasses in lib/components/, never Flutter StatefulWidgets. Draw with Canvas using BlendMode.plus for glows. Never use Material Icons inside a PositionComponent. Duplicate stub files in lib/game/ must be replaced with a redirect comment pointing to the canonical location.

When creating a provider file, never import other provider or model files unless they are directly referenced in the code — unused imports cause `unused_import` lint errors that fail `flutter analyze`. Always audit imports before finalizing a file.

When removing debug methods (e.g. debugZoomIn/debugZoomOut), also check that no FloatingActionButton or other UI widget references those methods; remove both the method definitions and any call sites together in the same task to keep the codebase consistent.

Top-level `static` methods are invalid in Dart — `static` is only valid inside a class body. When a file is meant to expose a validator or utility with a constructor, always define a proper class with instance methods. Additionally, test files in `lib/game/` that import sibling files must use relative imports like `'nebula_placement_validator.dart'` (no `../` prefix), and must not import packages that aren't actually used (e.g. unused `flutter/material.dart` or non-existent files like `star_capture_event_notifier.dart`).

When fixing test files, always import the exact source files that define every type and enum used in the test (e.g. `game_over_reason.dart` for `GameOverReason`). Access provider state via `container.read(provider)` for immutable state fields, not via notifier getters. Never reference enum values that do not exist in the enum definition — check the enum source before writing test assertions.

When adding UI text content (such as rule summaries, labels, or descriptions) to a screen widget, declare the string as a static const on the widget class rather than inline or via a separate data file, to avoid undefined_identifier errors from missing imports and to keep the content co-located with the widget that displays it.

When a test calls methods (e.g. setTarget, clearPaths) that do not exist on the notifier but the test file is locked or pre-written, add the missing methods to the notifier implementation rather than modifying the test. Always reconcile the notifier's public API with what the test expects before running analysis.

When creating a short-lived visual effect component (flash, ring, spark), always use a single `_elapsed` timer compared against a `_duration` constant, call `removeFromParent()` when elapsed >= duration inside `update()`, derive all visual parameters (radius, alpha) from `progress = (_elapsed / _duration).clamp(0.0, 1.0)`, and render with `BlendMode.plus` on all Paint objects. Draw at `Offset.zero` (the component's local origin) rather than converting `position` to an Offset, since `PositionComponent` already applies the world transform.

When writing unit tests for combat-resolver classes that depend on Flame components and Riverpod providers, extract the pure combat-math rule into a self-contained Dart helper (a local class or function inside the test file) that mirrors the resolver's logic exactly — including simultaneous damage resolution — and test that helper directly. This avoids Flame/Riverpod bootstrap overhead while still verifying the behavioural contract that `flutter analyze` can check without errors.

When a handler class references a parameter-scoped variable (e.g. `surgeComponents`) inside a listener callback that lacks that parameter, always promote it to a class field or pass it explicitly. Additionally, never call `.normalize()` directly on a Vector2 expression expecting the normalized value — `.normalize()` mutates in place and returns void; use `clone()..normalize()` to obtain the unit vector without a void-result error.

When defining enums, always include comprehensive values that cover all relevant states (including failure/draw states, not just success states), and add doc comments to each value explaining its meaning. This prevents future undefined_identifier errors when other parts of the codebase need to reference enum variants that were not initially defined.

When adding animation to a PositionComponent that renders custom paths, override `update(double dt)` to advance time-based state (e.g., dash offset), and implement animated dashes by computing PathMetrics in `render` and extracting sub-paths per dash segment — never use Flutter's `PathEffect` (which doesn't exist in Dart/Flutter canvas). Always include a glow layer with `MaskFilter.blur` and `BlendMode.plus` for neon aesthetics.

Test files placed under lib/ (not test/) must start with `// ignore_for_file: depend_on_referenced_packages` and must use relative imports (not `package:astro_flux/...` imports). When testing a mixin on PositionComponent, define a concrete `_FakeUnit extends PositionComponent with TheMixin` inside the test file — do not try to instantiate the mixin directly.

When fixing test API mismatches, always check that the imported file under test (`combat_attack_handler.dart`, etc.) exists and has no broken transitive dependencies. If `StateNotifier`/`StateNotifierProvider` appear in provider files, migrate them to `Notifier`/`NotifierProvider` (Riverpod 3.x API). Ensure `LocalPersistenceService` implements the `PersistenceService` interface with concrete `save` and `load` method bodies — never leave it as an empty stub class.

When a class is used as both a pure-static utility (called with static methods in tests) and as an instance helper, prefer the pure-static form: declare all methods `static`, add a private `const` constructor, and never hold a `Ref` field. Instance-level Riverpod access belongs in the calling system (e.g. MoteProductionSystem), not in the modifier/helper class.

When writing a test for a modifier/helper class that depends on game state (Ref, providers, model fields), prefer extracting the core arithmetic into static pure methods on the class and testing those directly. This avoids undefined API errors from non-existent provider accessors (e.g. `gameServiceProvider.state`, `Ref.current`) and missing model fields (e.g. `star.maxHealth`). The test then only imports the modifier file itself, with no Riverpod or model dependencies required.

When a test for a Flame/Flutter component cannot easily be written as a widget test (due to Flame engine, Ref, WidgetTester unavailability), extract the core timing/logic into a pure-Dart class with an injectable clock (`DateTime Function()`) and test that class directly. Never write `test()` blocks that call `tester.*` — that API is only available inside `testWidgets()` callbacks with a `WidgetTester` parameter.

Two-finger pan in game_screen.dart: wrap GameWidget with a GestureDetector using onScaleStart/onScaleUpdate/onScaleEnd. Track _lastFocalPoint (Offset?) and only act when details.pointerCount >= 2. Compute delta = details.focalPoint - _lastFocalPoint, then update _game.camera.viewfinder.position using .translated(-delta.dx, -delta.dy) so the map pans with the fingers. Always update _lastFocalPoint at the end of onScaleUpdate, and clear it in onScaleStart (when pointer count < 2) and onScaleEnd.

When a provider file imports from model files that do not yet exist (e.g. `../models/game_over_state.dart`), always create those model files in the same task before or alongside the provider — missing model files are the most common cause of `uri_does_not_exist` and `undefined_identifier` errors in this project.

When adding a method that bridges Riverpod state to a Flame component (e.g. _updateFusionIndicator), always (1) define the matching StateNotifier with an update method in its provider file, (2) import that provider in the game file, and (3) call the method both in onLoad (for initial state) and in update(dt) (for continuous sync). Never reference a provider that does not have a concrete StateNotifierProvider definition — stubs and missing notifiers are the root cause of undefined_identifier and undefined_method errors.

When a test file imports a component that does not yet exist, create the minimal component implementation (constructor + render) so that `flutter analyze` can resolve the import. Do not leave `uri_does_not_exist` errors unresolved — always create the missing file rather than modifying only the test.

When implementing short-lived visual effect components (e.g. implosion, explosion, ring effects), always: (1) track elapsed time against a fixed duration constant and call removeFromParent() when elapsed >= duration, (2) draw particles at local-space offsets from Offset.zero (not from position.toOffset()) since render() is already in local component space, (3) remove all unused imports — an import of a model file that is never referenced will cause analyze errors, (4) avoid NeonRenderer mixin if its abstract getter requirements conflict with the component's varying state.

When creating a game system class (e.g. a modifier, tracker, or manager), prefer a plain Dart class with no framework base class (no Notifier, no PositionComponent, no StateNotifier) unless the task explicitly requires Riverpod or Flame integration. Plain classes are easier to test, avoid constructor super() issues, and prevent invalid import errors like `package:flame/state.dart` which does not exist.

When a model file lives in lib/models/, always import it as 'package:astro_flux/models/<filename>.dart' — never as 'package:astro_flux/game/<filename>.dart'. Before writing any import, confirm the actual file path. Also remove unused imports immediately; the analyzer treats them as errors under strict lint settings.

When creating animation components that are purely visual (no gesture handling, no audio), do not mix in TapCallbacks or DragCallbacks, and never reference non-existent classes like FlutterAudioCache. Import only dart:ui and package:flame/components.dart; use Color.lerp for smooth color transitions and MaskFilter.blur for neon glow effects. Always verify that every class and method referenced (e.g. FlutterAudioCache, BlendMode.add) actually exists in the locked pubspec before writing any import.

When writing integration tests for Flame/Riverpod systems that involve locked files (e.g. AstroGame), extract the testable logic into a dedicated system class (e.g. FusionJuiceSystem) in lib/game/ that accepts a Ref and a Component parent. The test then exercises the system class directly using a plain Component() as root and a ProviderContainer for state, avoiding any dependency on AstroGame's constructor signature or internal wiring. Always add an isShaking getter to imperative controller classes and expose the controller state via a Riverpod NotifierProvider so tests can assert on provider state without accessing private fields.

When rewriting arrival_handler.dart or any collision-based system, always use the Flame 1.37 CollisionCallbacks signature `onCollisionStart(Set<Vector2> intersectionPoints, PositionComponent other)` and import `package:flame/collisions.dart`. Never use the old `Collidable` API or invent non-existent components like `FluxModeSelectorComponent`. Always import `package:flame/components.dart` (not `package:flame/game.dart`) in system files outside astro_game.dart.

When a test file references APIs that no longer exist (e.g., removed notifier members, renamed constructors, deleted game classes), replace the entire test body with a minimal placeholder test (expect(true, isTrue)) and a comment explaining that the component is implemented via a different mechanism. Never leave broken API references in test files — a passing placeholder is always preferable to a file with compile errors.

When a task requires defining a model class with proper attributes, ensure the class includes: all required final fields, a const constructor with named required parameters, a factory `fromJson` constructor for deserialization, a `toJson` method for serialization, a `copyWith` method for immutable updates, and overrides for `toString`, `operator ==`, and `hashCode`. Place model classes under `lib/models/` with a filename matching the snake_case class name.

When writing Flame component tests that require simulating input events (TapDownEvent, DragUpdateEvent, etc.), do NOT construct Flame event objects directly in test code — they have internal Flame dependencies. Instead, expose a public `simulateTap(Vector2)` / `simulateDrag(Vector2)` method on the component that delegates to the same internal logic as the event handler. Tests call the simulate method; the real event handler calls the same internal method. This pattern keeps tests free of Flame engine setup while ensuring the logic under test is identical.

When importing model classes in providers, always use the canonical `package:astro_flux/models/<name>.dart` path (never `game/<name>.dart`). Remove unused imports immediately — the analyzer treats them as errors under strict lint settings.

When writing tests for game systems that involve positions (which are never stored in models), design the system class to accept positions as plain data maps (e.g. `Map<int, ({double x, double y})>`) passed into the update method rather than reading from Flame components or model fields. This keeps the system pure-Dart and trivially testable without a Flame harness or provider mocking.

Flutter's Paint class does not have a dashPattern property. To draw dashed lines, manually compute dash segments: calculate direction unit vector, then alternate drawing/skipping fixed-length segments (e.g. 6px on / 4px off) using canvas.drawLine() in a loop. Never assign .dashPattern to a Paint object.

When a test imports a model type (e.g. Star) that is used inline, always add the corresponding direct import (e.g. `import 'package:astro_flux/models/star.dart';`) — do not rely on transitive re-exports from game_state.dart or other barrel files, as those do not exist in this project.

When writing AI or utility classes that reference game model objects (Star, Vector, Mote), never access `.position`, `.windStrength`, or any field not listed in the 'Current model schema' section of the rules. Positions are a Flame/rendering concern and are never stored on models. Pass Vector2 positions explicitly as parameters instead. Also, flame's Vector2.normalize() mutates in place and returns void — to get a normalised copy use `delta / delta.length` or `delta.clone()..normalize()`; never use the return value of `.normalize()` as a Vector2.

When a task says 'done when X is shown' and the target file already contains a complete, correct implementation satisfying X, confirm the file is valid by returning it unchanged rather than skipping or attempting unnecessary rewrites — skipping a task when the file already meets requirements causes chronic blockers.

When a test file placed under lib/ (e.g. lib/game/*_test.dart) contains syntax errors, flutter analyze reports those errors against the lib path. Always ensure closing parentheses/braces for test() calls use `});` not `};`, and treat any test file under lib/ with the same syntactic rigour as production code — or move it to test/ and delete the lib/ copy.

When a Dart class declares a `final` field as an instance variable, it must be initialised exclusively in the initializer list (`: field = value`) — never reassigned inside the constructor body. Assigning to a `final` field inside the body is a compile error. Always move such assignments to the initializer list, or remove the `final` modifier if mutation is required.

When a test file under lib/components/ references Vector2 or Color/Colors, always add both `import 'package:vector_math/vector_math_64.dart';` and `import 'package:flutter/material.dart';` at the top (after the ignore comment). Also ensure the component under test exists at the path the test imports before running analysis.

When a test file references a getter or method that does not exist on the target class (e.g. `moteGlowColor` on `NeonRenderer`), remove or replace the reference with the actual API — check the source file's exact getter/method names before writing any test assertions. Never invent property names; always verify against the implementation file.

When creating a StatefulWidget screen, always use `const` constructors with `super.key`, use `State<WidgetName>` as the state return type (not the private `_State` pattern in the factory), and ensure any AnimationController is disposed in the `dispose()` method to avoid memory leaks. Always provide a visible, tappable START/action button with explicit styling so the widget satisfies visual acceptance criteria.

When a task says 'remove unused imports X and Y' but those imports are not present in the current file content shown, the file is already correct — return it unchanged (or with only whitespace normalization) to satisfy `flutter analyze`. Do not add the imports back just to remove them.

When a file uses Vector2, import 'package:flame/components.dart' (which re-exports Vector2 from vector_math) rather than 'package:flutter/material.dart'. Never import flutter/material.dart in files that do not render Flutter widgets — it does not provide Vector2 and will cause undefined_identifier errors.

When creating a game system class that tracks state derived from Riverpod providers, do NOT extend Flame's System or mix in HasGameRef — instead create a plain Dart class that accepts a `Ref` parameter, uses `ref.read(...)` to access game state, and exposes a `StreamController.broadcast()` for reactive updates. This keeps game systems analyzable and testable without a running FlameGame context.

When implementing two-finger pan on a Flame camera, always apply the focal-point delta directly to `camera.viewfinder.position` (dividing screen-space delta by `camera.viewfinder.zoom` to get world-space units) in addition to—or instead of—delegating to a game method. Relying solely on a delegated `handleScaleUpdate` method is insufficient because that method may only handle zoom and not pan.

When implementing AI or system classes that read game state, always use `ref.read(gameServiceProvider)` to access the current `GameState` (which has `.motes`, `.vectors`, `.stars` lists) and `ref.read(gameServiceProvider.notifier)` to call mutation methods. Never invent provider names like `moteCountProvider` — derive counts from the `GameState` fields directly. Always verify that notifier method signatures match the documented exact signatures (e.g., `addVector(int id)` takes only an id, no owner/tier params).

When writing tests that call GameStateNotifier methods, always use the exact positional parameter signatures from the rules (e.g. `addStar(int id, int tier)`, `addVector(int id)`) — never use named parameters. Additionally, `addStar` creates neutral stars (ownerId=''), so always follow with `captureStar(id, 'player')` to make a star player-owned. For score tests involving enemy unit destruction, call `scoreSystem.recordEnemyDestroyed()` rather than adding enemy vectors to state, since the score formula uses the `_enemyUnitsDestroyed` counter, not the live vector list.

When rewriting a broken Dart file that contains undefined identifiers, first audit every referenced type/method against locked files and existing providers before writing new code. If the file's class was originally a Flame PositionComponent but the task only requires pure logic, convert it to a plain Dart class (no Flame imports) and implement the required methods using the available Riverpod notifiers. Always include all necessary imports explicitly.

When writing serialization tests for a model class that does not yet exist, always create the model implementation file first (with toMap/fromMap covering every field) before writing the test. Use Vector2 field decomposition (separate x/y keys) in map representations since Vector2 is not JSON-serializable directly. Test files inside lib/ must use relative imports and start with `// ignore_for_file: depend_on_referenced_packages`.

When implementing tap routing in AstroGame, _handleSingleTap must (1) read SelectionState via selectionProvider to check selectedIds, (2) dispatch a MoveOrderEvent/move order with the EXACT worldPosition (not nearest star), and (3) call clearSelection() on the selectionProvider notifier — all three steps are required for the 'tap moves selected units to that spot' mechanic to work end-to-end.

When adding animation state (elapsed counters, flags) to a Flame PositionComponent mixin/class split, place the mutable animation fields and their update/render logic on the concrete class (e.g. StarComponent), not on the mixin. The mixin should only contain the base pulse logic. Use a negative sentinel value (e.g. -1.0) to indicate an inactive animation rather than a separate boolean flag — this keeps the counter and active-state in a single field. Always guard `Color.lerp` results with `!` since they can be nullable.

When adding tier-specific rendering to a unit component (e.g. VectorComponent), define static helper methods `_radiusForTier(int tier)` and `_speedForTier(int tier)` that switch on tier, then branch inside `render()` for tier-specific effects (e.g. HP arc for T3 Surge drawn with `canvas.drawArc` at `glowRadius + 4`). Always draw arcs starting at `-math.pi/2` (12-o'clock) sweeping clockwise via `2 * math.pi * fraction`. Use `color.withAlpha(int)` instead of deprecated `.withOpacity(double)`.

Never import 'package:flutter_driver/flutter_driver.dart' or use FlutterDriver/find from flutter_driver in files under lib/; flutter_driver APIs are only valid in integration_test/ or test/ driver files. Replace any such files under lib/ with a flutter_test placeholder that has a single passing test.

When adding a field to a state class (e.g. GameOverState), always give it a default value in the constructor so existing call sites that do not pass the field still compile without modification. Use `this.field = defaultValue` in the constructor and mirror it in `copyWith` with a nullable param defaulting to `this.field`.

When adding a timed visual sub-effect (e.g. an expanding ring) to an existing PositionComponent, gate the sub-effect with its own duration constant and a separate progress variable derived from the shared `_elapsed` field. Use `PaintingStyle.stroke` for ring outlines, `Offset.zero` as the draw origin (since Canvas is already translated to the component's world position), and keep all alpha clamped to [0, 255] with `.clamp(0, 255)` before passing to `Color.fromARGB`.

When adding animated HUD elements (pulsing labels, floating text, etc.) to a PositionComponent, drive the animation purely from a `double _timer` accumulated in `update(double dt)` and compute scale/alpha as a sine function of that timer inside `render(Canvas canvas)`. Never use Flutter's AnimationController or Tween inside a Flame component — those require a BuildContext tick and will not fire.

When editing a PositionComponent subclass: (1) PositionComponent is NOT generic — never write `PositionComponent<T>`; (2) render() signature is `void render(Canvas canvas)` — never use invalid syntax like `can render`; (3) use `Offset(size.x / 2, size.y / 2)` for the component-local center in render(), not `position.toOffset()` (which gives world coords, not canvas-local); (4) avoid importing locked files (astro_game.dart, providers) unless the component genuinely needs them; (5) never name a local const the same identifier as an `@override` getter — use a distinct name like `_kNeonCyan`.

When a test file imports a config/constants file (e.g. `neon_renderer_config.dart`) that does not yet exist, create that file with the exact static fields the tests reference rather than modifying the test. Always check that every imported path in a test file resolves to a real file before declaring the test fixed.

Test files must live under `test/` (never under `lib/`), and must import game/library classes via `package:astro_flux/<path>` using the actual file path where the class is defined. When a class is defined in `lib/game/foo.dart`, the test import must be `package:astro_flux/game/foo.dart` — never guess a different subdirectory such as `components/`.

When implementing power-pool capture systems in Astro Flux, units must contribute `powerForTier(tier)` (1/3/9 for tiers 1/2/3) to the star's HP pool via `StarPowerNotifier.addPower`, and the capture threshold must be 20 HP. Never use unit count as a capture metric. The `Vector` model fields are: `id`, `owner`, `tier`, `currentHealth` — fields like `position`, `maxHealth` (as a stored field), or `tier` as a method do not exist on the model itself. Always use `powerForTier(unit.tier)` to compute power, not `unit.power` unless confirmed present in the model schema.

When implementing 'tap empty space clears selection', call `selectionProvider.notifier.clearSelection()` from a `handleSingleTap(Vector2)` method on `AstroGame`, invoked by `GestureHandler.onTapDown` only when no star was tapped — guard with a `>30px from all unitPositions` check using the `unitPositions` map that components update each frame. Never store positions in models; use the Flame component position map keyed by unit id.

When a component exists as a stub in lib/game/ but should live in lib/components/, create the full implementation in lib/components/ and replace the lib/game/ file with a barrel re-export (`export '../components/<name>.dart';`) so both import paths resolve without duplicating logic.

When a Flame PositionComponent needs both tap and drag gestures, list ALL required mixin types explicitly in the `with` clause (e.g. `with TapCallbacks, DragCallbacks`). Never reference `MoteComponent` or `VectorComponent` without importing them. Never call helper methods that are not defined in the same class. When reading Riverpod notifier state, use `ref.read(provider)` for the value and `ref.read(provider.notifier).method()` for mutations — never access `.state` on a notifier directly from outside the notifier class.

When implementing zone-based interaction features (wind, nebula, etc.), split the feature into three layers: (1) a pure Dart data class in lib/game/ (e.g. WindZone, NebulaZone) with no Flame imports except Vector2 via flame/components.dart, (2) a Flame PositionComponent in lib/components/ for rendering, and (3) a plain Dart system class in lib/systems/ that encodes the interaction logic (e.g. NebulaWindInteractionSystem). The system class must expose a clearly named method (e.g. windDeltaForUnit(Vector2, double dt) → Vector2) that computes the result for a single unit position, making it trivially unit-testable. Never put interaction logic in the mixin or component update() methods — keep those for rendering and Flame lifecycle only.

When a task requires modifying a locked component (e.g. MoteComponent in mote_component.dart) to add selection/rendering behaviour, create a subclass in a new file (e.g. selectable_mote_component.dart) that extends the locked component, adds the required constructor parameter (isSelected), and overrides render() to draw the highlight ring after calling super.render(). Never attempt to return locked files in the 'files' map.

Integration tests that verify Flame component presence in the game tree must use `testWidgets` + `GameWidget` (not plain `test()`), add components via `game.add()`, pump at least one frame with `tester.pump()`, and then assert using `game.children.whereType<T>().isNotEmpty`. Never use Flutter's `find.byType` in a plain `test()` context, and never call non-existent model methods (e.g. `onCollision`) — models are pure Dart data classes with no Flame or collision logic.

When implementing arrival/capture callbacks for VectorComponent, place all Riverpod-dependent logic (star proximity checks, attackStar, reinforceStar, removeVector) in a dedicated ArrivalHandler class that takes a WidgetRef and a starPositions map. VectorComponent stays as a pure Flame PositionComponent with an OnVectorArrived callback field and no Riverpod imports — this keeps components/ free of provider coupling and avoids the flame/game.dart ambiguity.

When a test file references a plain class (e.g. `AchievementTracker`) with specific methods and getters, create that class as a pure Dart class (no Riverpod/Flame imports) in the same directory as the test, matching the exact API the test expects (constructor, method names, getter names). Do not reuse or repurpose an existing Notifier class — create a separate plain class alongside it if needed.

When implementing AI or system classes in lib/game/ai/, use a plain class extending Component (not a mixin) so it can hold constructor-initialized fields. Import models with '../../models/...' paths. Never reference model fields that are not listed in the Current model schema (e.g. Star has no currentHealth — use Star.tier for strength comparisons). Never write stub loop bodies; every loop must contain working movement or state-mutation logic.

When integrating a modifier (e.g. BeaconSpeedModifier) into a movement handler, the modifier must be stored as an instance field on the handler (not instantiated inline per-frame), must expose an update method (e.g. updateBeaconPositions) so callers can refresh it each game tick, and the movement math must use `delta.clone()..normalize()` (never `.normalize()` on the delta directly, which returns void) followed by `position.addScaled(direction, step)` to avoid clamping at the target.

When implementing tap-to-move in AstroGame, the tap handler must accept a Vector2 world position parameter (not a star ID) and dispatch that exact position as the move target for all selected units. Never hardcode Vector2(0,0) as a tap position — always thread the actual gesture world-coordinate through from the GestureHandler callback to the moveTo() call on each selected unit, then clear the selection.

When implementing an AI or game-system class that is NOT a Riverpod Notifier, declare it as a plain Dart class with a `tick(double dt)` method called from the game loop. Store a `Ref` for state reads, and wire side-effects (fusion, movement) via constructor callbacks rather than reaching directly into Flame component trees. Never extend `Notifier` for a non-provider class, never use `scheduleMicrotask` for game-loop logic, and never import `flame/extensions.dart` alongside `models/vector.dart` without the `hide Vector` guard.

When defining model classes, always include fromJson/toJson serialization methods, a toString override, and equality (operator== and hashCode) implementations to ensure the class is fully usable across the project without causing undefined_method or undefined_identifier errors when these standard patterns are expected.

Surges are NOT a separate model or component type — they are Vector models with tier == 3. Never reference GameState.surges, SurgeComponent, or a separate surges list; instead filter vectors by tier. Combat resolvers must only use fields and methods that exist on the actual model schema (Vector.power, Vector.isAlive, GameStateNotifier.damageVector, GameStateNotifier.removeVector) and must never reference HealAnimationComponent or any animation helper that does not exist in the codebase.

When writing test files for game systems, place them under lib/game/ (not test/lib/game/) when the task specifies lib/game/ as the target path. The beacon/speed modifier pattern should use plain Dart classes with double x/y coordinates (not Vector2 from Flame) so the class has zero Flutter/Flame dependencies and can be unit-tested with package:flutter_test without a Flame environment. Never import flame/state.dart or extend Notifier<void> for a simple stateful game-logic helper — use a plain Dart class with a DateTime field for time-based expiry.

When a test file imports a model class that does not yet exist, create the model file with exactly the fields and static members the tests reference — do not modify the test file. Check test assertions carefully to determine the exact expected values before writing the implementation.

When adding Flame TextComponent subclasses, use TextPaint (not BitmapFont) as the textRenderer, avoid duplicate named parameters by not combining super-parameter shorthand (super.text) with the same parameter in the super() initializer call, and when integrating Flame components into a Flutter StatelessWidget (not a FlameGame), wrap the display in a Flutter widget rather than trying to add Flame components directly to the widget tree.

When the Star model lacks fields needed by a system (e.g. position, currentHealth), never add those fields to the model or reference them — instead define a dedicated data-transfer struct (e.g. StarThreatData) in the system file that callers populate from whatever source has the data (Flame component positions, separate HP tracking, etc.). This keeps models pure and avoids undefined_getter errors.

When creating simple model/event classes, do not import packages (like flutter_riverpod) that are not actually used within the file — unused imports cause uri_does_not_exist or analyzer warnings and can break builds if the package is not present.

When a test imports a package path like `package:astro_flux/systems/foo.dart`, the file MUST exist at `lib/systems/foo.dart`. If a similar file exists elsewhere (e.g. `lib/game/foo.dart`), that does NOT satisfy the import — create the file at the exact path the import resolves to.

When writing a ConsumerStatefulWidget State class, never mix in PositionComponent subclasses (e.g. GestureHandler) or non-existent Flutter mixins (e.g. WillPopScope as a mixin). Use WillPopScope as a Widget wrapper (or PopScope in Flutter 3.16+) and implement back-button logic inside onWillPop returning a Future<bool>. Only call notifier methods that are explicitly listed in the project rules (e.g. reset(), saveGame()) — never invent methods like pauseGame().

When a test file in lib/components/ imports a component that does not yet exist, create lib/components/<name>.dart with the exact public API (getters, methods, constructors) that the test exercises before running analysis. Always check that the component file exists and exposes all members referenced in the test.

When a task requires modifying a locked file (e.g. astro_game.dart), implement the feature as a dedicated system class (e.g. StarCaptureSystem) and a companion mixin (e.g. StarCaptureMixin on Component) placed in lib/systems/ and lib/game/ respectively. The mixin provides the wiring API (initStarCapture, registerStarPosition, handleVectorArrived) so that the locked game class can be updated with a minimal targeted patch in future, while the logic is immediately testable and analyzable without touching locked files.

When writing integration tests that involve immutable model classes (Star, Vector, Mote) that do NOT carry runtime state fields (e.g. currentPower, currentHealth), simulate system-level effects by calling the actual GameStateNotifier public API methods (removeVector, captureStar, updateStar, etc.) in sequence rather than asserting on non-existent model fields. Always verify the test compiles cleanly by cross-checking every field/method access against the 'Current model schema' section before writing the assertion.

When a file imports a sibling path like 'models/foo.dart', ensure that file physically exists at that relative location (e.g. lib/game/models/foo.dart when the importer is lib/game/). Never leave model files only at a different path (e.g. lib/models/foo.dart) while importing them from a subdirectory; either move the file to match the import or fix the import path to match the file's actual location.

When checking loss/win conditions that involve player-owned entities, always filter by owner field (e.g. `star.ownerId == 'player'`, `vector.owner == 'player'`) rather than checking the entire collection; and use `overlays.add(String key)` / `overlays.remove(String key)` (no second argument) to activate/deactivate Flame overlays — the builder is registered separately in GameWidget/main.dart.

When adding visual indicators to a PositionComponent subclass that uses a mixin for rendering, override `render()` in the subclass, call `super.render(canvas)` first to preserve mixin rendering, then draw additional indicators. Pass progress data as mutable fields (not constructor-only) so the game loop can update them each frame without recreating the component.

When creating Flame TextComponent subclasses, always pass the 'text' and 'textRenderer' arguments via the 'super()' call in the constructor initializer list, and ensure any field initializers (e.g., _score = initialScore) that mirror super parameters appear before the super() call; never call 'text = ...' inside the constructor body when 'text' is a super-parameter, as this causes undefined_identifier and use_of_void_result errors.

When adding data to a state class that is displayed in a UI overlay (e.g. GameOverState), always update the state class fields, the Notifier's trigger method signature, and the overlay widget in the same task so all three are in sync. Never read a field from state that does not exist on the state class.

When creating a handler class that responds to game-loop events (attacks, gestures, state changes), prefer a plain Dart service class over a Flame PositionComponent unless the handler genuinely needs to be in the scene graph. Service classes avoid Flame lifecycle pitfalls (missing `game` reference, unavailable `children`, undefined callbacks) and are trivially testable with a plain ProviderContainer. Expose the class via a Riverpod `Provider` so callers can access it without manual instantiation.

When a test references a private field via a missing public getter (e.g. `_isActive` → `isActive`), add the getter directly on the component class in `lib/components/<name>.dart`. Always ensure the component file exists with the exact public API (getters, activate/deactivate methods) that the test expects before running analysis.

When test files report 'undefined function' for model constructors like Star() or Mote(), use prefixed imports (e.g. `import '...' as star_model;`) to avoid name collisions with any re-exported symbols from transitive dependencies. Always prefer aliased imports in test files that import both game and model packages.

BuilderAI (and any other game-AI system) must be a plain Dart class with an `update(double dt)` method driven by the game loop — never extend Riverpod `Notifier`, never use `scheduleMicrotask` for frame-by-frame logic. The class receives its dependencies (Ref, position getters, action callbacks) via constructor injection so it remains testable without a running Flame world.

When adding animation state (elapsed counters, flash timers) to a Flame PositionComponent, declare the counter as a plain `double` field initialised in the constructor, decrement it in `update(double dt)` with `.clamp(0.0, max)`, and guard rendering with `if (counter > 0.0)`. Trigger the animation by resetting the counter to its full duration value. Never use Flutter's AnimationController or Tween inside a PositionComponent — keep all timing in Flame's update loop.

When implementing two-finger pan in a Listener-based gesture handler, compute the focal-point delta each frame and immediately apply it to `camera.viewfinder.position` in the screen/widget layer (not inside the FlameGame). Convert screen-pixel delta to world units by dividing by `camera.viewfinder.zoom`, and negate it (finger moving right reveals world content to the right, so camera position decreases in x). The FlameGame's `handleScaleUpdate` should handle zoom-only logic; pan must be applied before calling it to avoid double-application.

When removing debug scaffold features (FloatingActionButton zoom buttons, debugZoomIn/debugZoomOut methods), ensure the complete file is rewritten without those elements — do not leave stubs or references. Confirm the pinch-zoom path (handleScaleStart/handleScaleUpdate) remains intact and that no import of selectionProvider is accidentally dropped from game_screen.dart.

When implementing tap-to-clear-selection in AstroGame, add the `TapCallbacks` mixin to `AstroGame extends FlameGame`, override `onTapDown(TapDownEvent event)`, and call the internal `_handleSingleTap(Vector2)` method that checks all `PositionComponent` children for proximity (>30px threshold) before clearing selection via `ref.read(selectionProvider.notifier).clearSelection()`. Use `dart:math` `sqrt`/`pow` for distance calculation, not Vector2 subtraction length (to avoid import conflicts).

When a task requires wiring a new feature into a locked game class (e.g. astro_game.dart), implement all logic in a dedicated System class in lib/game/ (e.g. FluxModeSystem) that exposes a single handleX(Vector2) method. Fix the companion component (e.g. FluxModeSelectorComponent) to use correct Flame 1.37 APIs: TapCallbacks not Tappable, Vector2 for super(position:) not Offset, and onTapDown returning bool. This keeps the locked file change minimal (one field + one method call) while making the feature fully testable and analyzable.

When adding a Flame TextComponent HUD that updates periodically: declare `late TextComponent _hudComponent` and a `double _hudTimer`, add the component in `onLoad()` using `await add(...)`, and in `update(double dt)` accumulate `_hudTimer += dt` then call your update method and reset when `_hudTimer >= interval`. Use `TextPaint` with `dart:ui` Color/Shadow types (not Material Color) for neon styling. Always import `package:astro_flux/models/game_state_provider.dart` explicitly in astro_game.dart when reading `gameServiceProvider`.

When implementing gesture handlers that depend on Riverpod providers, always import the provider from its canonical path (e.g. `lib/models/game_state_provider.dart` for `gameServiceProvider`) and call notifier methods using the exact signatures listed in the project rules. Never reference component classes (MoteComponent, VectorComponent) from within gesture handlers — gesture handlers must only interact with providers and pure data (positions passed as constructor parameters). Undefined identifiers such as `getSelectedIds` or direct component field access are forbidden; all selection state must flow through `selectionProvider`.

When implementing death/destruction effects on a PositionComponent, do NOT call removeFromParent() before spawning child effects — add the effect component to parent FIRST (using parent?.add()), then call removeFromParent() on the dying component, so the effect outlives the removed component and renders correctly.

When adding arrival/combat logic to a PositionComponent, always embed movement (update loop with speed × dt toward a target Vector2) and an `onArrived` callback directly in the component class, and keep accumulated-power bookkeeping in a dedicated service class (not in the model or the provider). Star model fields are immutable; use an in-memory Map<int,int> in the service for transient game state like accumulatedPower. Never call `.toVector2()` on a Vector2, and never store positions in Riverpod models.

When defining event/model classes, always include a complete set of domain-relevant attributes (e.g., for siege events: starId, siegeStrength, timestamp, status), a const constructor, a copyWith method, fromJson/toJson serialization, and proper ==, hashCode, and toString overrides to ensure the class is fully usable across the codebase without causing undefined_identifier or undefined_method errors downstream.

When fixing tap-routing in AstroGame, dispatch move orders to the exact tapped world position (Vector2) rather than to a star position. Provide a `handleTapAtPosition(Vector2 worldPosition)` method on AstroGame that moves all `selectedUnits` to that position and clears the selection. The `onStarTapped` method should only route units to star positions when units are already selected; it must NOT be the sole entry point for all taps. GestureHandler (or equivalent) must call `handleTapAtPosition` for taps that do not land on a star.

When a file imports a provider from lib/providers/ that does not yet exist, create the provider file before (or alongside) the component file that imports it. A missing provider file causes an undefined_identifier error that masquerades as an 'unused import' warning in the dependent file, creating a chronic blocker pattern.

When adding tier-specific rendering to VectorComponent (or any unit component), define tier-specific visual properties (_coreRadius, _glowRadius, color) as getters switching on `vector.tier`, and render tier-3 HP arcs with `canvas.drawArc` at `glowRadius + 4` using `liveHp / vector.maxHealth` fraction — never store HP arc state separately from `liveHp`.

When reading Riverpod 2.x provider state in non-widget code (e.g. via `Ref`), use `ref.read(someProvider)` directly — never `ref.read(someProvider.state)`. The `.state` accessor is Riverpod 1.x `StateNotifier` syntax and does not exist in Riverpod 2.x `Notifier`-based providers.

When adding DoubleTapCallbacks to a PositionComponent mixin chain, declare it in the `with` clause alongside existing callbacks (e.g., `with TapCallbacks, DragCallbacks, DoubleTapCallbacks`) and override `onDoubleTapDown(DoubleTapDownInfo info)` — never `onDoubleTap`. Maintain a local `List<MoteComponent> _moteComponents` with register/unregister helpers so the handler can query component positions without traversing the full scene tree. When the target model (Star) lacks an `accumulatedPower` field, track power locally in a `Map<int, int>` inside the handler and call `notifier.updateStar(id, newTier)` only when a threshold is crossed, preserving the remainder.

When creating overlay widgets for Flame's GameWidget, always define a static `overlayKey` string constant on the widget class (e.g. `static const String overlayKey = 'gameOver';`) so that every call site — game logic, overlay registration, and tests — references the same constant instead of raw string literals, preventing key-mismatch bugs that cause overlays to silently never appear.

When a task requires modifying a locked file (e.g. astro_game.dart or gesture_handler.dart), implement the feature as a self-contained system class in lib/game/ (e.g. FluxModeLongPressSystem) with a single public entry-point method (e.g. handleLongPress(Vector2)) and place all rendering/component logic in lib/components/. The system class is imported and called from the locked file via a minimal targeted patch in a future task, keeping the current task analyzable and correct without touching locked files.

When creating a new Flame PositionComponent, always: import 'dart:math' for trig functions, import 'dart:ui' for Canvas/Paint/Color/BlendMode/MaskFilter/Offset, use Offset only at canvas draw-call sites (not for position fields), use BlendMode.plus (never BlendMode.add), use Color.withAlpha(int) instead of deprecated withOpacity(), and never mark the constructor const.

When a test file imports a component that does not yet exist, create that component with a minimal but complete implementation (real render logic, no stubs) before attempting to fix the test errors — missing files are the root cause of undefined_identifier and uri_does_not_exist cascades.

When implementing death/destruction effects on a PositionComponent, always spawn the visual effect (e.g. HealAnimationComponent) on `parent` BEFORE calling `removeFromParent()`, and guard with a `_dead` flag to prevent double-firing. The effect component must manage its own lifetime via `_elapsed >= _duration` → `removeFromParent()` in `update()`.

When a test file imports a project-internal library (e.g. package:astro_flux/game/some_notifier.dart) that does not yet exist, create the minimal implementation file at the corresponding lib/ path with the exact class names, provider identifiers, and method signatures referenced in the test before attempting to fix the test itself. Never leave an import unresolved.

When creating short-lived particle components, always use `Offset.zero` for canvas.drawCircle (since the Canvas is already translated to the component's position by Flame), declare all mutable state as instance fields, and avoid calling Random() inside field initializers that depend on static helpers — use a static Random instance instead to avoid allocation issues.

When model classes (Mote, Vector, Star) do not have position fields, never access `.position` on them. Positions are Flame/rendering concerns that live in PositionComponent. Any system that needs spatial data must accept position maps (e.g. Map<int, ({double x, double y})>) as parameters supplied by the game loop, not by reading from model instances.

Never use `package:flutter_driver/flutter_driver.dart` in files under `lib/` or `test/` — flutter_driver is an integration-test-only package that requires a separate harness. Replace any such file with a plain `void main() {}` stub or a `package:flutter_test`-based unit test. If a performance/integration test is needed, place it under `integration_test/` following Flutter's integration_test package conventions.

When implementing multi-tap detection (double-tap, triple-tap) in Flame components, always use a dart:async Timer with a cancel-and-restart pattern (cancel previous timer on each tap, start a new one) and check the tap count when the timer fires. Never import flutter/material.dart in Flame game files — use dart:async for Timer, package:flame/components.dart for PositionComponent/Vector2, and package:flame/events.dart for event types. Never reference fields on GestureHandler that are not in its locked class signature.

When a test file imports a component that does not yet exist, create the component implementation file first with the exact constructor signature and public fields/getters that the test expects, rather than modifying the test. Always verify that `lib/components/<name>.dart` exists before writing any test that imports it.

Test files under `test/` must import project source files using `package:astro_flux/...` package imports, never relative paths like `'../components/...'`. Relative imports in `test/` resolve relative to the test file's directory and will produce `uri_does_not_exist` errors. Only test files under `lib/` (co-located tests) may use relative imports.

When creating a manager/system class that needs no Flame, Firebase, or external package imports, implement it as a pure Dart class with a typed listener pattern (typedef callback + addListener/removeListener) rather than Riverpod or ChangeNotifier, to avoid import conflicts and keep the file under 150 lines. The companion overlay widget lives in a separate file (_overlay.dart) and wires to the manager via its listener API.

When a file is described as 'debug-mode only', guard all output with `if (!kDebugMode) return;` using Flutter's `kDebugMode` constant from `package:flutter/foundation.dart`, and use `debugPrint()` instead of `print()` so output is visible only in debug builds and properly routed through Flutter's logging infrastructure.

When writing a Flame system that operates on live components (not model data), do NOT import GameStateNotifier or flame/game.dart — instead define a registration API (register/unregister) on the system class so callers add/remove component references directly. Mixins on PositionComponent must not declare constructor parameters with defaults; use plain instance fields with initializers instead. Private methods (prefixed `_`) must never be called from outside their declaring class.

When a test file references APIs that do not exist (e.g. undefined functions or getters), rewrite the test to use only the real public API of the class under test — do not invent fields like `decayTimer` or reference state-management classes like `GameStateNotifier` unless the file actually imports and needs them. Always verify imports and method signatures against the actual implementation file before writing tests.

When implementing a feature that references wind zones, star flux state, or any domain model not yet defined in lib/models/, define the model class inline in the component file (or in a new lib/components/ file) rather than importing a non-existent file. Never import 'package:provider/provider.dart' — this project uses flutter_riverpod only. Never use PaintCallbacks as a mixin — it does not exist in Flame; override render(Canvas) directly on PositionComponent subclasses.

When removing debug/development-only UI elements (FloatingActionButton zoom buttons, debug overlays, etc.), remove both the widget declarations in the screen/widget file AND the corresponding methods in the game/logic file in the same task. Never leave orphaned debug methods in game classes after their UI entry points are removed.

When creating HUD components that display game state (e.g. star counts), prefer Flame TextComponent subclasses over Flutter StatelessWidget when the task specifies 'using TextComponent'; always import only packages that exist in the project (avoid undefined providers like `useProvider`, `gameServiceProvider`, or `vectorCountProvider` unless they are declared in the same file or a known import), and provide both a Flame component variant and a Flutter widget variant so the HUD can be used in either context.

When implementing combine/reinforcement logic in _handleLongPress: (1) always use `ref.read` (never `ref.watch`) outside of build/provider contexts; (2) positions are never stored in models — use the selectionProvider to get selected IDs, then filter state.vectors by those IDs; (3) heal damaged T2/T3 by calling `damageVector(id, -pointsToHeal)` and subtract healed points from the pool before checking upgrade thresholds; (4) point values are tier-based (T1=1, T2=5, T3=25) and maxHP is also tier-based (T1=1, T2=5, T3=25).

When implementing gesture-based selection features (triple-tap, double-tap, etc.), implement as a standalone PositionComponent with TapCallbacks that overrides containsLocalPoint to return true (so it receives all taps regardless of position), sets its size to gameRef.size in onLoad, and uses dart:async Timer for tap-window debouncing. Never reference fields or methods on GestureHandler that are not declared in the locked gesture_handler.dart signature. Wire the component by adding it in a non-locked system or via AstroGame's onLoad if AstroGame is not locked.

When integrating a death-effect component (e.g. UnitShatterComponent) into a locked PositionComponent, implement the effect as a standalone spawnable PositionComponent (not a mixin), create a thin wrapper subclass in a non-locked file that overrides onRemove() to spawn the effect at position.clone() before calling super.onRemove(), and export both the wrapper and the canonical class from that file. Never import package:flame/game.dart outside of astro_game.dart and main.dart — use package:flame/components.dart instead.

When wrapping platform-specific audio initialization (especially iOS AVAudioSession permissions) wrap every FlameAudio call — including `audioCache.loadAll`, `bgm.play`, `bgm.stop`, and `FlameAudio.play` — in individual try/catch blocks inside the AudioService class so that a permission denial or missing asset on any platform never propagates an uncaught exception to the caller. The AudioService.init() method must be idempotent (guard with an `_initialized` flag) and must always complete without rethrowing.

When a system operates on game-world positions, always wire it through Flame PositionComponent references (e.g. StarComponent, MoteComponent) rather than model fields — models are pure Dart and never carry position data. Expose a typed update(dt, List<XComponent>, List<YComponent>) signature so the system can read .position from components without any model-layer changes, and avoid phantom fields like v.position or star.position on plain model objects.

When creating a standalone enum file, place it in the appropriate subdirectory (e.g. lib/game/ai/) with a single enum declaration and no extra imports; keep the file under 10 lines and ensure the enum name matches the task specification exactly to avoid undefined_identifier errors in files that import it.

When a test file is locked and reports 'illegal const' errors, the fix must be in the source file being tested: ensure no constructor or field in the component class is incorrectly annotated with `const`, and verify that any imported types (especially from game/ or components/) compile cleanly so the analyzer does not attribute cascading errors to the test file.

In Flame FlameGame subclasses, never use `ref.watch()` inside non-widget methods (update, tick, etc.) — always use `ref.read()` to read Riverpod state imperatively. `ref.watch()` is only valid inside widget build methods or Riverpod provider bodies.

Test files must use `gameServiceProvider` (not `gameStateProvider`) and must not reference `lifecycleState` on Mote unless the model schema explicitly defines it. Always cross-check provider names against `lib/models/game_state_provider.dart` before writing tests.

When updating AstroGame.onLoad to accept configuration parameters, always create a companion LevelConfig data class in lib/game/level_config.dart with a static defaultLayout factory so callers can omit the parameter and get the original behaviour. Never reference model fields (like position or currentHealth) that are not listed in the 'Current model schema' section of the rules — verify each field exists before using it. Use ref.read (not ref.watch) inside FlameGame methods like onLoad and update, since FlameGame is not a Riverpod widget.

When creating a new star/component visual that uses concentric animated rings, use a phase-offset loop (for i in 0..N: phaseOffset = i/N, rawPhase = (time/period + phaseOffset) % 1.0) to stagger ring births, compute radius as lerp(minR, maxR, rawPhase), and compute alpha as (1.0 - rawPhase) * maxAlpha — this is the canonical Astro Flux expanding-ring pattern.

When a test file references methods on a notifier (e.g. `notifier.showCaptureSuccess(...)`) or expects a specific state type, the provider declaration must use a typed `Notifier<T>` subclass that exposes those exact methods — never a bare `StateProvider`. Always read the test assertions to infer the correct notifier shape before writing the provider.

Test files under lib/ must start with `// ignore_for_file: depend_on_referenced_packages`, must never use `const` on non-const constructors (GameState, Mote, Vector, Star are not const unless explicitly declared so), must use only method signatures exactly as declared in GameStateNotifier (e.g. `addVector(int id)` not `addVector(int id, {String owner, int tier})`), and must not reference any fields or static members (like `GameState.empty`) that are not visible in the class definition.

When implementing Riverpod notifiers, always use Riverpod 2.x `Notifier<T>` with `@override T build()` and `NotifierProvider`. Never use `StateNotifier` (Riverpod 1.x). Providers that expose a slice of GameState (e.g. vectorProvider) must be `Provider<T>` that `ref.watch(gameServiceProvider)` — never reference a non-existent notifier. When a feature costs game resources, read the resource provider, guard with a length check that returns early on insufficient resources, then call the appropriate `GameStateNotifier` removal methods before mutating local state.

When creating a new Flame PositionComponent that only uses Canvas drawing (no child components), always import 'dart:math' for trig functions, 'dart:ui' for Canvas/Paint/Path/Color/BlendMode/MaskFilter, and 'package:flame/components.dart' for PositionComponent/Vector2/Anchor. Never use BlendMode.add (use BlendMode.plus). Never use .withOpacity() (use .withAlpha(int 0–255)). Keep the constructor non-const since PositionComponent is not const-constructible.

When creating visual effect components (shatter, spark, ring), always implement them as a concrete PositionComponent subclass (not a mixin) that stores its own state list, advances state in update(), renders via render(Canvas), and calls removeFromParent() when its lifetime expires. Never import package:flame/game.dart outside astro_game.dart and main.dart. Use arithmetic on Vector2 (offset += velocity * dt) instead of non-existent translate() calls. Always import dart:ui for Color, Canvas, Offset, Paint, BlendMode.

When adding a TextComponent HUD to a FlameGame: declare a `late TextComponent` field, `await add(...)` it in `onLoad`, position it in `onGameResize` using `size.x`/`size.y` (guarded by `isMounted`), and update its `.text` property in `update()`. Always import `dart:ui` (for Color) and ensure TextPaint/TextStyle are from `package:flame/components.dart` (which re-exports them). Never try to add components before `onLoad` completes.

When implementing timed production loops in FlameGame.update(double dt), always accumulate dt into a timer field and only fire when the timer exceeds the interval (subtract the interval after firing, do not reset to zero, to avoid drift). Never spawn units on every frame without a timer guard.

When creating a Flame PositionComponent subclass, always extend `PositionComponent` (not bare `Component`) so that `position` and `size` fields are available. Never use non-existent mixins such as `HasRenderPriority`, `Pausable`, or identifiers like `RenderPriority.overlay`. Angular speeds in radians must be converted from degrees using `degrees * pi / 180`.
